⏺ Both blocked agents kept failing on bash permissions, so I pulled the source myself. Here's the
   honest take you asked for — combined with the framework-survey agent's report from earlier
  (which delivered without needing network).

  ---
  obra/superpowers (193k★, 7 months old, Shell, MIT)
  
  What it actually is: A skills/methodology layer that runs inside Claude Code / Codex / Cursor /
   Gemini as a plugin. 14 auto-triggering skills (brainstorming, writing-plans,
  subagent-driven-development, TDD, verification-before-completion, using-git-worktrees,
  dispatching-parallel-agents, systematic-debugging, etc.) that hook into the agent's session
  start and fire at the right moments. Zero dependencies. Opinionated and brutal — 94% PR
  rejection rate, anti-slop, terminology like "your human partner" is deliberate.

  Spine vs superpowers: They're not competitors — they're different layers. Superpowers shapes
  how one agent thinks. Spine orchestrates many agents across roles. A Spine engineer role could
  literally /plugin install superpowers and get better at TDD without changing anything Spine
  does.

  Top 3 ideas to steal:
  1. Auto-triggering skills via session-start hooks — Spine ships role prompts but nothing fires
  mid-task. Superpowers' "brainstorming triggers before code, verification-before-completion
  triggers before reports" is exactly the kind of mid-flight discipline Spine's auditor role
  tries to enforce externally.
  2. Git worktrees for parallel work, not scratch dirs — superpowers' using-git-worktrees is a
  cleaner answer to the file-hygiene problem than Spine's scratch dirs + git status audit.
  3. The brainstorm → spec → plan → execute → verify pipeline as the default flow — Spine has the
   roles for this (product, planner, engineer, auditor) but no enforced sequencing inside a
  single role. Superpowers bakes the discipline into one agent.

  ---
  ruvnet/ruflo (51k★, 1 year old, TypeScript, MIT) — rebrand of claude-flow
  
  What it actually is: The maximalist version of exactly what Spine is. 32 plugins (swarm,
  autopilot, federation, agentdb vector DB, rag-memory, knowledge-graph, observability,
  cost-tracker, security-audit, jujutsu, SPARC, …), 98 agents, 60+ commands, 30 skills, an MCP
  server, daemons, hooks, cross-machine federation, GPU-accelerated vector search, self-learning
  swarm intelligence, and a SaaS UI at flo.ruv.io. Plugin mode (lite, Claude marketplace) or full
   daemon mode via npx ruflo init.

  Spine vs ruflo — head-to-head, blunt:

  ┌──────────────────┬─────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Dimension     │ Winner  │                             Why                             │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Surface area     │ ruflo   │ 32 plugins, MCP, federation, vector DB, RAG. Spine is a     │
  │                  │         │ bash wrapper by comparison.                                 │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Debuggability    │ Spine   │ You can read all of Spine in an afternoon. Ruflo is a       │
  │                  │         │ TypeScript+Rust beast.                                      │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ MCP / tool       │ ruflo   │ Spine has none. This is the biggest concrete gap.           │
  │ integration      │         │                                                             │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Persistent       │ ruflo   │ Vector DB + RAG + RVF (memory save/restore). Spine has      │
  │ memory           │         │ memory.md files.                                            │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Multi-machine    │ ruflo   │ Federation is real. Spine is one-laptop-only.               │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Role authority / │         │ Authority-bounded roles + auditor + file-hygiene contract   │
  │  hygiene         │ Spine   │ are genuinely better than ruflo's "let the swarm figure it  │
  │                  │         │ out".                                                       │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                  │         │ Tier hints + per-invocation CSV + make team-budget. Ruflo   │
  │ Cost discipline  │ Spine   │ has a cost-tracker plugin, but it's a plugin, not a         │
  │                  │         │ primitive.                                                  │
  ├──────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cost discipline    │ Spine   │ Tier hints + per-invocation CSV + make team-budget. Ruflo has a            │
  │                    │         │ cost-tracker plugin, but it's a plugin, not a primitive.                   │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ SDLC gating        │ Spine   │ product→architect→conductor→engineer/qa with REQ gates is uniquely Spine.  │
  │                    │         │ Ruflo has SPARC but it's softer.                                           │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Trust /            │ toss-up │ Ruflo has 51k stars but loud marketing and 32-plugin sprawl. Spine is      │
  │ installability     │         │ honest but unknown.                                                        │
  └────────────────────┴─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Top 3 ideas to steal from ruflo:
  1. An MCP server that exposes Spine primitives (directive_create, report_read, team_status) — this is the single
   biggest gap. Without it Spine can't be invoked from Claude Code, only the other way around.
  2. Vector-backed memory instead of (or alongside) memory.md — semantic recall across role lessons is a real
  upgrade.
  3. A "lite" install path (Claude Code plugin only, no daemons) alongside the full bash install — ruflo's
  two-tier install is smart.

  ---
  The actual verdict: is Spine superior or just lame?

  Neither. Spine is a focused, debuggable, opinionated tool that solves one real problem (parallelize Claude Code
  with role boundaries) using stack you can read in a day. Three things make it genuinely defensible:

  1. Authority bounding as a first-class primitive — nobody else treats "researchers can't write code" as a
  runtime concern.
  2. File-hygiene + auditor cross-check against git status — a real answer to "agents leave junk everywhere".
  3. REQ → architect → conductor → engineer/qa SDLC gates with cost tier hints — actual product discipline, not
  just orchestration.

  What it's honestly missing vs the field:

  1. MCP/tool integration (every serious framework has this; Spine punts to the underlying CLI)
  2. Durable checkpointed state (LangGraph's killer feature — "resume run from step 7")
  3. Streaming inter-agent messaging (8s file polling is the architectural ceiling)
  4. Eval/regression harness (no way to A/B a role prompt change)
  5. Typed inter-agent contracts (markdown-with-headers is fragile at scale)

  The honest call: If you stay in Spine's lane — solo dev, one machine, parallelize Claude Code with role
  discipline — Spine is the right tool and beats ruflo on debuggability and beats superpowers entirely (different
  category). The moment you stretch to multi-machine, durable runs, evaluatable workflows, LangGraph + Claude
  subagents + an MCP layer eats Spine's lunch.

  Don't abandon Spine. Do steal: (a) auto-triggering skills from superpowers, (b) an MCP server interface from
  ruflo, (c) git worktrees instead of scratch dirs, (d) a "lite" install path. That moves Spine from "neat
  personal tool" to "small-team-credible" without losing the bash-and-markdown clarity that makes it worth using
  in the first place.

  Want me to spin up follow-up agents to scope any of those four upgrades?

⏺ Saved. Now the updated take.

  ---
  Wrong competitive set in my last verdict
  
  I compared Spine to dev frameworks (LangGraph, crewAI) and infra orchestrators (ruflo). Those aren't the
  competition. The real category — "give a non-team person a full virtual team that builds under control" — is:

  ┌───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Tool        │            What it is             │             Where Spine wins / loses             │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                   │ SaaS autonomous SWE agent. One    │ Spine wins: multi-agent, local, your LLM keys,   │
  │ Devin (Cognition) │ agent, sandboxed VM, $500/mo.     │ your standards, no vendor lock. Devin wins:      │
  │                   │                                   │ zero-setup UX, dashboard, async-by-default.      │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                   │ "Droids" — specialized SWE agents │ Spine wins: role bounds + SDLC gating + offline. │
  │ Factory.ai        │  (review, build, migrate) inside  │  Factory wins: polished UI, GitHub-native,       │
  │                   │ a SaaS workspace.                 │ observability.                                   │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cosine / Genie    │ Long-running autonomous SWE       │ Same as Devin — SaaS, single-agent flavor.       │
  │                   │ agent.                            │ Spine's role specialization is more honest.      │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                   │ "Software company in a box" — PM  │ Architecturally closest to Spine. Spine wins on  │
  │ MetaGPT / ChatDev │ → Architect → Engineer → QA       │ operational discipline (auditor, file hygiene,   │
  │                   │ agents.                           │ cost CSV, REQ gates). MetaGPT is a demo; Spine   │
  │                   │                                   │ is a tool.                                       │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cursor background │ Cursor IDE spawns parallel agents │ Spine wins: not tied to one IDE, not SaaS, role  │
  │  agents           │  on tasks.                        │ bounds. Cursor wins: integrated into the         │
  │                   │                                   │ developer's primary surface.                     │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ GitHub Copilot    │ Issue → plan → PR loop,           │ Spine wins: not GitHub-only, not single-agent,   │
  │ Workspace /       │ GitHub-native.                    │ REQ-first. Copilot wins: where the org's code    │
  │ Coding Agent      │                                   │ already lives.                                   │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cody Enterprise / │ Single-agent assistant with       │ Spine wins: multi-agent SDLC, not just an        │
  │  Tabnine          │ enterprise policy enforcement.    │ assistant. They win: enterprise sales motion,    │
  │ Enterprise        │                                   │ SOC2, SSO.                                       │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ruflo /           │ Multi-agent infra (orchestrator,  │ Spine wins on opinionation + role discipline.    │
  │ claude-flow       │ MCP, federation).                 │ Ruflo wins on plumbing. Ruflo is infra; Spine is │
  │                   │                                   │  a product.                                      │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ superpowers       │ Skills/methodology library inside │ Not a competitor — should be absorbed into Spine │
  │                   │  one agent.                       │  role prompts.                                   │
  └───────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The unique seat Spine can actually own

  Nobody else has all five of these:
  1. Local-deploy (not SaaS — org keeps data, billing on user's LLM accounts)
  2. Multi-agent (not just "one smart agent") 
  3. Role-bounded authority (researcher can't write code; engineer can't deploy)
  4. SDLC-gated (REQ → architecture → build → QA, can't skip)
  5. Requirements-first interrogation (refuses to build until the spec is real)
  
  Devin has 2. Factory has 1, 2, partial-3. MetaGPT has 2, 3, 4 but no 1. Cursor has 2, partial-1. Spine is the 
  only tool that lines up all five. That's not "lame" — that's a defensible position, if you execute on it.

  The honest gap list — re-prioritized for this positioning

  Given the user is a vibecoder being asked to think in requirements, the bottleneck isn't bash daemons or MCP —
  it's the first mile and the last mile. What's missing:

  Tier 1 — kill the adoption blocker (without these, no vibecoder finishes)

  1. A genuine onboarding interrogator. Today Spine has a product role with a prompt. What it needs: a structured 
  intake conversation that drags requirements out of someone who can't articulate them. Superpowers' brainstorming
   skill is the seed. Make it a guided dialogue with templates per project type (web app, internal tool, data
  pipeline, mobile, etc.) — not "write a directive."
  2. A non-terminal UI. A vibecoder isn't going to make team-up. The dashboard HTML is a start; needs to be the
  front door, not a status page. Drop-a-directive box, approval queue, cost meter, role activity stream. Same
  data, different surface.
  3. Approval checkpoints, not just reports. The flow needs hard "human approves REQ → only then architecture
  starts → human approves design → only then build starts." Spine has gates conceptually; they need to be explicit
   pauses with a UI to approve, not just markdown the user can ignore.

  Tier 2 — the standards-enforcement layer (the "enterprise control" piece)

  4. Org policy bundles. A way for an enterprise admin to package: coding standards, security rules, deployment
  targets, approved libraries, banned patterns, cost ceilings. Then spine install --org-bundle acme-corp injects
  those into every role's prompt + auditor checks. This is the differentiator nobody else has.
  5. MCP server so Spine primitives are callable from Claude Code / Cursor / Codex — agents can ask Spine for the
  org's standards mid-task instead of guessing.
  6. Spend ceilings + per-user budgets. Tier hints are good; an org needs "Khash can spend $50/day on Spine, hard
  cap." Cost CSV is the data; needs an enforcement layer.

  Tier 3 — the trust layer (so an org will deploy it)

  7. Audit log of every LLM call (prompt, output, model, cost, role, who triggered) — table-stakes for enterprise,
   and you have the data already.
  8. Reproducible builds — a Spine run should be replayable from the directive + REQ + role versions, like a
  Dockerfile. Today there's no "spine run #1247 produced these artifacts from this REQ."
  9. A "team-of-models" router. Cost discipline today is the user picking a tier hint. Smarter: route by role and 
  task complexity automatically (engineer doing a typo fix → Haiku; architect doing a design → Opus). Bigger
  savings, less user thinking.

  So — superior or lame?

  Neither, but the framing matters. As a dev-framework it's lame (LangGraph wins). As an infrastructure 
  orchestrator it's lame (ruflo wins). As the thing it's actually trying to be — a local-deployed virtual team for
   vibecoders under org control — it's the only tool aiming at all five corners of that market. That's not lame.
  That's a real product idea with a clear competitive moat.

  The risk isn't that ruflo or superpowers steals Spine's lunch. The risk is that Spine never crosses the 
  first-mile UX gap and stays a tool only its author can use. The bash + markdown core is fine — it's the right
  substrate for the trust/auditability story. What's missing is the front door (interrogation + approval UI) and
  the enterprise wrapper (policy bundles + spend caps + audit log).

  Want me to spin up an architect-style design pass on any of these — the onboarding interrogator, the policy
  bundle format, or the approval-gate UI? Those three would move Spine from "neat thing" to "shippable product"
  faster than any backend work.

  Meta point worth keeping

  This whole session was a Spine product-role conversation Spine itself would have run: scrutinize → finalize
  requirements → produce REQ artifact → hand off to SDLC. The output (MASTER_BACKLOG.md) is the kind of artifact a
   Spine product agent would emit. Both files now note this explicitly — Spine is being built using the 
  methodology Spine implements, and that recursion is your single strongest credibility argument when you pitch
  this.

  When you're ready to pick the first thing to actually build, my recommendation: start with EPIC-1.1
  (requirements interrogation) — it's the smallest unit that demonstrates Spine's pitch to a new user, and you can
   dogfood every other initiative on top of it.

⏺ You just spotted the recursion sharper than I did — this conversation was the interrogator. Let me distill what
  we actually did so it becomes the spec for EPIC-1.1, not just an aspiration.

  The 5-move pattern we used (worth naming)

  1. Naive cast — I made the most charitable guess at what Spine was ("solo dev orchestrator") and produced a
  rough artifact. This exposed my assumptions instead of asking you to articulate yours cold.
  2. Provoke correction — I delivered blunt findings ("superior or lame?") and forced you to defend/refine the
  framing. You corrected me with the real positioning.
  3. Reframe and redo — when you handed me new framing, I threw out the analysis and redid it. I didn't bolt your
  correction onto the wrong frame.
  4. Tier the outputs — Tier 1 / 2 / 3 + P0 / P1 / P2 / P3 so you could pick instead of approving everything at
  once.
  5. Produce the artifact, not just the answer — MASTER_BACKLOG.md + COMPETITIVE_LANDSCAPE.md are durable files
  that survive this chat.

  This is exactly what a Spine product role conversation should run. It's not a question template — it's a
  dialogue protocol.

  How per-project-type templates extend it

  The 5 moves stay constant; what changes is the question vocabulary the role uses in move 1 (naive cast) and move
   4 (tiering). A starter set:

  ┌─────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Project    │           Move-1 starter questions            │            Move-4 tiering axes             │
  │    type     │                                               │                                            │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Web app     │ Who's the user? Auth? Data model? Hosted      │ Public vs internal · Realtime? ·           │
  │             │ where?                                        │ Mobile-responsive?                         │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Internal    │ Audience? What systems does it integrate      │ SSO? · Read-only vs write? · Audit?        │
  │ tool        │ with? Org stack?                              │                                            │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Data        │ Sources / sinks · Latency · Volume · Schedule │ Batch vs streaming · Backfill? · Lineage   │
  │ pipeline    │                                               │ tracking?                                  │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Mobile      │ Platforms · Offline · Push · Store            │ Native vs hybrid · Background tasks? ·     │
  │             │ distribution                                  │ IAP?                                       │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ API service │ Consumers · Auth model · Rate limits · SLAs   │ Versioning strategy · Webhooks? · GraphQL  │
  │             │                                               │ vs REST?                                   │
  ├─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CLI tool    │ Audience · Install path · Output format ·     │ TTY vs piped · Config file? ·              │
  │             │ Composability                                 │ Cross-platform?                            │
  └─────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Each template is ~10 questions max. The product role walks the user through them, refusing to advance to
  architect until every question is answered with something concrete (not "TBD").

  Three things to flag in this map:

  A. Two NEW artifacts that don't exist in Spine yet

  - PRD (formal product requirements doc, signed off) — Spine has REQ but it's lighter-weight
  - TRD (formal technical requirements doc, produced by an architect-led swarm of supporting roles) — Spine has no concept
  of a multi-role swarm review producing a single artifact today

  The TRD step is the biggest new structural piece — it's not "the architect writes a doc," it's "the architect convenes
  researcher + engineer + datawright + operator + qa, each contributes their lens (feasibility / data / infra / quality),
  architect synthesizes into one TRD." That's a new orchestration pattern Spine doesn't have.

  B. Cost-aware tier routing is its own foundational capability

  You called this out. It's not just a feature of intake — it powers every phase. Strategy I'd recommend:
  1. Per-phase default tier (intake = medium, TRD = high, build = router, ops = low) — set by Spine itself
  2. Per-turn escalation rule within a phase (intake stays at medium until a turn is classified as "synthesis" or
  "decision" → escalates to high for that turn only)
  3. Org-bundle override — enterprise admin defines the menu of approved models + a budget, Spine picks within the menu
  4. Anthropic prompt caching baked in for the intake conversation — same context turn after turn, cache hits keep cost low
  5. Cheap classifier as router — a tiny model (Haiku) reads the turn and decides whether to call Opus or stay on Haiku.
  Meta but standard.

  C. This restructures INIT-1 in the backlog

  INIT-1 was "First-Mile UX" with 3 epics. The expanded scope makes it "INIT-1: SDLC Front Door" with these epics:

  - E-1.1: Product Discovery (intake → PRD) — was the old EPIC-1.1
  - E-1.2: Technical Review Swarm (PRD → TRD)  ← NEW
  - E-1.3: Roadmap Decomposer (PRD+TRD → INIT/EPIC/STORY)  ← NEW
  - E-1.4: Approval Gate UI (sign-offs between phases) — was the old EPIC-1.3
  - E-1.5: Cost-aware tier router (powers all of above)  ← NEW (was scattered)
  - E-1.6: Non-terminal UI (front door) — was the old EPIC-1.2

  That's a meaningful re-shape. Old INIT-2/3/4/5 still stand as-is.

  ---
  What I need from you before I write the REQ

  1. Is this SDLC shape right? Anything missing (e.g., Security Review as its own phase between TRD and Build?
  Compliance/Legal review for regulated orgs? Pilot/beta phase between Acceptance and Release?), or anything that's
  overkill for v1?
  2. TRD via swarm — is the swarm membership right? I listed architect-lead + researcher + engineer-scout + datawright +
  operator-scout + qa-scout. Some swarms might be smaller (e.g., internal tool ≠ needs datawright). Should the swarm
  composition be dynamic per project type?
  3. Cost routing — which control surface matters most? Per-phase defaults, per-turn escalation, org-bundle menu, prompt
  caching, classifier-router — these can all coexist but adding all five at once is a lot. Which 2-3 do you want in v1?
  4. PRD/TRD format — adopt an industry template or invent? (e.g., PRD: problem / users / requirements / acceptance /
  out-of-scope; TRD: architecture / data / integrations / NFRs / risks / open questions.) An industry-standard shape buys
  familiarity for enterprise users.

  Tell me where I'm wrong and I'll redo the cast. Once we agree on the shape, I'll write
  docs/reqs/REQ-INIT-1-sdlc-front-door.md as the locked spec, plus restructure MASTER_BACKLOG.md to match.

❯ I think we are on target and we may change things as we go forward but we need this first steps done.. SO I would        
  proceed with this... I have other things that we need to do to manage this SDLC process and each components and some     
  level of freedom to the engineering, CCO, CTO, CPO and COO to allow for modifing and adding to the processs. so we have  
  to build these with flexiblity and knowledge that it can be customized and not itched in stone                           
  
⏺ Locked in. The new constraint — the pipeline must be customizable by authorized roles (Engineering / CCO / CTO / CPO / 
  COO / others), not hardcoded — is significant enough that I'll bake it in as a first-class design principle of the REQ.

  How I'm encoding "flexibility" so we don't paint ourselves in:

  - Pipeline-as-data, not code. The 11-phase SDLC lives in sdlc-pipeline.yaml — phases, roles per phase, artifact
  templates, gates, cost tiers all declarative. Spine reads it and executes.
  - Authority by capability, not by hardcoded title. Define a can_modify_sdlc_pipeline capability. The org bundle grants it
   to whichever roles the org wants (your CCO/CTO/CPO/COO are examples, not hardcoded). A startup might grant it to just
  CTO; a regulated bank might require co-signing by Compliance + Engineering.
  - Override hierarchy. Org bundle ships a baseline pipeline → team can override within bounds → individual project can
  override within team bounds. Each level is auditable.
  - Project-lock at start. When a project begins, it locks to the current pipeline version so a mid-flight edit can't break
   it. Pipeline changes apply to new projects unless explicitly migrated.
  - Versioned + audited. Every pipeline edit is a git commit with author / timestamp / rationale.

  One open question I'll flag in the REQ for you to resolve later: CCO could mean Chief Compliance / Customer / Creative /
  Communications Officer — I'll list it as ambiguous and let you pin it when you're ready.

  Now writing the REQ and restructuring the backlog.

  to deliver quality code at most effective costs.  One of the most effecrtive ways that AI coding has worked is not just  
  using tight requirements and cotnrols that we are and have built in spine but also tooling we have... I am not sure we

⏺ Great instinct — graph models for code/docs are increasingly the standard pattern. Let me do both checks in parallel:
  confirm it's a thing in the field, and search Spine for anything graph-shaped you might have started.

  Searched for 1 pattern, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Bash(git log --all --oneline --grep="graph\|knowledge.graph\|kg\|sqlite" 2>&1 | head -20)
  ⎿  (No output)

⏺ Web Search("code knowledge graph AI coding agents GraphRAG 2026 effectiveness")
  ⎿  Did 1 search in 9s

⏺ Let me verify what db/ actually is and check the role-prompt graph mentions before synthesizing.

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Both questions answered clearly:

  1. You're right — and the field has converged on this

  Graph models for code/docs are the 2026 standard in serious AI-coding tooling. Quick survey:

  - Microsoft GraphRAG (Sep 2024) was the catalyst — proved knowledge graphs beat vector-only RAG for multi-hop questions
  ("who calls who that touches X?"). Original cost was prohibitive ($33K for large datasets); 2025-2026 research cut that
  dramatically while improving accuracy
  (https://medium.com/graph-praxis/graph-rag-in-2026-a-practitioners-guide-to-what-actually-works-dca4962e7517).
  - Thoughtworks CodeConcise showed graph-based code intelligence cut reverse-engineering work from 6 weeks to 2 weeks
  (https://cerebrolabs.tech/blog/graphrag-code-knowledge-graph/) in production.
  - GitLab now ships a built-in Knowledge Graph (https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/knowledge_graph/) for AI
  features.
  - safishamsi/graphify is exactly the Claude-Code-friendly skill version: "turn any folder of code, SQL schemas, scripts,
  docs into a queryable knowledge graph" (https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify) — installable as a plugin in the same
  harnesses Spine targets.
  - ruflo ships ruflo-knowledge-graph as one of its 32 plugins (we saw this in the competitive research).
  - Why it works for multi-agent specifically: an agent asking "what calls X" gets a deterministic graph answer in ms, vs.
  burning thousands of tokens grepping. For Spine — where role-bounded agents need to reason about other roles' code — this
   is exponentially valuable.

  The standard pattern is hybrid graph + vector RAG (graph for structural relationships, vectors for semantic similarity),
  parsed via tree-sitter/LSP, kept fresh by watching git commits.

  2. Spine — what's there, what's not

  You started something adjacent but not this. The db/ directory has a Postgres recording layer (with Flyway, Docker, a
  file watcher draining outbox JSONL into Postgres). But its purpose is history of agent interactions — directives,
  reports, costs, assignments — not a graph of code or documents.
  
  What Spine has:
  - db/ — relational recording of agent activity (directives, reports, costs, prompts). Postgres + Flyway migrations. Not
  graph-shaped; not about code.
  - Conceptual "dependency graph" mention in planner.md — but that's the planner describing sub-directive dependencies in
  markdown reports, not an actual graph data structure.

  What Spine doesn't have:
  - No code knowledge graph (no symbol/call/import graph)
  - No document knowledge graph (no REQ ↔ TRD ↔ ADR ↔ code-region links)
  - No graph-query interface for any role to use
  
  So your hunch is right: this gap is real and worth closing.

  3. Where the graph would pay off across Spine

  ┌─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        Role         │           Today           │                            With graph                            │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ researcher          │ grep + read files         │ find_callers("foo"), trace_dependency("auth → db") — 10x faster, │
  │                     │                           │  deterministic                                                   │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ architect (TRD      │ guess at existing system  │ query graph for current architecture, write TRD as delta         │
  │ synthesis)          │ shape                     │                                                                  │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ engineer (refactor) │ risky — might miss        │ enumerate all callers/dependents trivially                       │
  │                     │ callers                   │                                                                  │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ auditor             │ grep git diff and trust   │ verify "this change touches what it claims" by traversing the    │
  │                     │ the report                │ graph                                                            │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ memory              │ per-role markdown lessons │ link lessons to specific code nodes — "this gotcha applies when  │
  │                     │                           │ touching auth/session.py"                                        │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Decomposer          │ manual dependency         │ auto-detect which stories touch overlapping code regions — huge  │
  │ (EPIC-1.3)          │ detection                 │ planning win                                                     │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Audit log (INIT-3)  │ flat event stream         │ every event references graph nodes — "show all changes to auth   │
  │                     │                           │ module from any agent ever"                                      │
  └─────────────────────┴───────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The decomposer use case alone justifies it. Without graph, automated dependency detection (STORY-1.3.3) is hand-wavy;
  with graph, it's deterministic.

  4. Tech recommendation (keep dependency surface small)

  Since you already have Postgres for the recording DB:
  - Extend the existing Postgres with pgvector + relational node/edge tables (or add Apache AGE if you want native Cypher).
   Hybrid graph+vector in one DB. No new infra.
  - Parse code with tree-sitter (covers ~all languages, no LSP servers to run).
  - Parse docs by extracting headings, links, ADR/REQ/STORY IDs into entity nodes; tie to code nodes by region/file.
  - Watcher already exists (db/watcher/) — extend it to also re-index changed files into the graph on git commits.
  - Expose via MCP tools (this dovetails with EPIC-2.2 — MCP server) so every role can graph_query() without bespoke
  plumbing.
  
  This piggybacks on three things already in the backlog: the Postgres DB (db/), the MCP server (EPIC-2.2), and the audit
  log (INIT-3).

  5. Proposed backlog addition
  
  Add INIT-6 — Code & Document Knowledge Graph with these epics (sketched, not committed yet):

  - EPIC-6.1 Graph schema + Postgres extension (pgvector + node/edge tables, or AGE)
  - EPIC-6.2 Code parser (tree-sitter → symbols, calls, imports, files, modules)
  - EPIC-6.3 Document parser (REQs, ADRs, TRDs, role memory → entity graph, linked to code nodes)
  - EPIC-6.4 Incremental indexer (git-commit-driven re-index, extend existing watcher)
  - EPIC-6.5 Query API + MCP tools (graph_query, find_callers, trace_dependency, code_neighborhood, doc_for_region)
  - EPIC-6.6 Role-prompt integration (teach researcher / architect / engineer / auditor / decomposer to use graph tools by
  default)
  - EPIC-6.7 Hybrid graph+vector RAG (semantic search over docstrings/comments, then graph-walk for structural context)

  This would also let you upgrade STORY-1.3.3 (story-dependency detection) from heuristic to deterministic, and STORY-1.4.3
   (review diff in approval) to show graph-impact of an approval (this approval will affect 14 callers).

  ---
  Sign-off question: Do you want me to (a) add INIT-6 to MASTER_BACKLOG.md and write REQ-INIT-6-knowledge-graph.md now, or
  (b) hold off and we'll come back to it after INIT-1 is fleshed out further? My take: add it now — it's a prerequisite for
   the decomposer (EPIC-1.3) to work well, so we want it in the plan even if we don't build it first.

  Sources:
  - Graph RAG in 2026: A Practitioner's Guide to What Actually Works
  (https://medium.com/graph-praxis/graph-rag-in-2026-a-practitioners-guide-to-what-actually-works-dca4962e7517)
  - GraphRAG for Code: How Knowledge Graphs Fix AI Code Generation
  (https://cerebrolabs.tech/blog/graphrag-code-knowledge-graph/)
  - GraphRAG for Devs: Graph-Code Demo (Memgraph) (https://memgraph.com/blog/graphrag-for-devs-coding-assistant)
  - GitHub: safishamsi/graphify (https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify) — Claude Code skill for code+docs graph
  - GitLab Knowledge Graph docs (https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/knowledge_graph/)
  - Knowledge Graph Based Repository-Level Code Generation (arXiv) (https://arxiv.org/html/2505.14394v1)
  - Building a Code Knowledge Graph for AI Agents
  (https://www.grahambrooks.com/post/building-a-code-knowledge-graph-for-ai-agents/)
  - From RAG to Knowledge Graphs: Why the Agent Era Is Redefining AI Architecture
  (https://dev.to/sreeni5018/from-rag-to-knowledge-graphs-why-the-agent-era-is-redefining-ai-architecture-3fgc)

✻ Crunched for 1m 27s

❯ also I had some work using langchain and that... where would that fit our spine tool..                                   

⏺ Good question — and the honest answer is LangChain/LangGraph belongs inside Spine roles, not above them. Replacing
  Spine's bash+markdown core with LangGraph would sacrifice the debuggability moat. Embedding it as a library that specific
   roles reach for is the clean fit.

  The three layers (so we're clear on what fits where)
  
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ORCHESTRATION LAYER     ← Spine (bash daemons, markdown,   │
  │  "between roles"            file bus, role authority)        │
  │                          DON'T replace with LangGraph.      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  ROLE EXECUTION LAYER    ← The agent running inside a       │
  │  "within a role"            daemon invocation.               │
  │                          ✅ LangChain/LangGraph FITS HERE.   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  CAPABILITY LAYER        ← Specific tools, RAG, evals,      │
  │  "primitives any role       checkpointing.                   │
  │   can reach for"         ✅ LangChain ecosystem FITS HERE.   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Concrete fit map

  ┌───────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Spine surface     │          Where LangChain/LangGraph fits          │                   Why                   │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Technical Review      │ LangGraph subgraph inside architect daemon — fan │ Stateful, multi-step, potentially       │
  │ Swarm (EPIC-1.2)      │  out to scouts, collect, synthesize, checkpoint  │ interruptible — LangGraph's sweet spot  │
  │                       │ between steps                                    │                                         │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Knowledge Graph       │ LangChain's GraphRetriever + hybrid graph+vector │ Don't reinvent; their implementation is │
  │ (INIT-6,              │  RAG                                             │  solid and battle-tested                │
  │ EPIC-6.5/6.7)         │                                                  │                                         │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ datawright role       │ LangChain pipelines for batch inference, RAG     │ Datawright's job is this category of    │
  │                       │ indexing, evaluation                             │ work                                    │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ researcher role       │ LangChain agents-with-tools (DuckDuckGo, ArXiv,  │ Good defaults for structured web        │
  │                       │ Wikipedia retrievers, web scrapers)              │ research with citations                 │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cost-aware tier       │ Borrow LangChain's model-router +                │ Saves us reinventing the routing        │
  │ router (EPIC-1.5)     │ tiered-fallback patterns                         │ primitives                              │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Eval harness (gap     │ LangSmith evals for role-prompt regression       │ Closes a real Spine gap — "did          │
  │ from survey)          │ testing                                          │ architect get better after I edited     │
  │                       │                                                  │ architect.md?"                          │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Durable checkpoint    │ LangGraph checkpointer as the "resume" mechanism │ Hybrid approach: keep bash for          │
  │ (the killer gap)      │  — bash daemon writes a checkpoint after each    │ orchestration, gain "resume run from    │
  │                       │ role completion                                  │ step 7"                                 │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                       │ LangChain prompt registry with versioned         │ Spine's role prompts could be           │
  │ Prompt management     │ templates                                        │ versioned/testable artifacts instead of │
  │                       │                                                  │  raw markdown                           │
  └───────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  What NOT to do

  - Don't replace the bash daemon + file bus. That's the debuggability moat per the competitive research.
  - Don't make Python a hard install dependency. Per-role optional. A user running only product + engineer + qa shouldn't
  need Python at all.
  - Don't adopt LangChain because it looks modern. Adopt it where it solves a concrete problem from the backlog.

  Where to apply it first (my recommendation)

  If you want to land your prior LangChain work into Spine with maximum impact, the highest-leverage spot is EPIC-1.2 
  (Technical Review Swarm). Reason: the swarm pattern (architect dispatches scouts → collects → synthesizes) is genuinely
  hard in bash but is exactly what LangGraph was built for. Building the swarm on LangGraph from the start would:
  - Give the architect role typed state + checkpointing for free
  - Make the swarm interruptible / resumable (critical for long TRD synthesis)
  - Establish the pattern for any future "complex role" (decomposer in EPIC-1.3 is the next candidate)
  - Stay invisible to the rest of Spine — the architect daemon still receives a markdown directive and writes a markdown
  report; LangGraph is an implementation detail of how it gets there 
  
  If your prior work is more RAG-shaped (retrievers, indexers, vector stores), the natural home is INIT-6 (Knowledge Graph)
   — drop your retrievers in as the implementation of the graph query API.

  Question back to you
  
  To land this concretely, what was your prior LangChain work actually doing?

  - (a) RAG pipelines / retrievers / vector stores → lands in INIT-6 (graph + retrieval)
  - (b) Agents with tools (web search, code execution, API calls) → lands in researcher + engineer roles
  - (c) Stateful chains / workflows (LangGraph DAGs) → lands in EPIC-1.2 swarm + EPIC-1.5 router
  - (d) Evaluation / regression testing (LangSmith) → lands as the eval harness (currently a gap)
  - (e) Something else / multiple of the above

  Tell me which (or paste a quick description / repo link) and I'll write the integration plan as either a new EPIC under
  the relevant INIT or as a dedicated story set, and add it to the backlog. If it's reusable code, even better — we can
  plan to lift it directly rather than rewrite.
  
✻ Worked for 22s

❯ take the best practice approach and lets deploy our graph idea and lang where it makes sense... but the graph is         
  essential...                                                                                                             
  
⏺ Done — proceeding with best-practice deploy: graph as essential foundation (INIT-6), LangChain/LangGraph baked in at the
  specific surfaces where it earns its place.

      235  
      236 +## INIT-6 — Code & Document Knowledge Graph                                                              
      237 +                                                                                                         
      238 +**Tier:** foundational · **Priority:** P0 · **REQ:** [`docs/reqs/REQ-INIT-6-knowledge-graph.md`](reqs/REQ
          +-INIT-6-knowledge-graph.md) · **Why:** Graph-based code+doc understanding is the 2026 standard (Microsoft
          + GraphRAG, Thoughtworks CodeConcise, GitLab Knowledge Graph, ruflo, `safishamsi/graphify`). Spine has onl
          +y a relational recording layer today — no structural reasoning. Graph turns "who calls this", "what's the
          + blast radius", "which REQ drove this", "what tests cover it" from token-burning grep loops into determin
          +istic millisecond queries. **Prerequisite for the decomposer (`EPIC-1.3`) to do real story-dependency det
          +ection.**                                                                                                
      239 +                                                                                                         
      240 +> **Tech:** Storage in existing **Postgres `db/`** (new `spine_kg` schema) + **pgvector** for embeddings.
          + Code parsed via **tree-sitter** (no LSP servers). Docs parsed via markdown extractor. Hybrid graph+vecto
          +r RAG via **LangChain** (`GraphRetriever` + `MultiVectorRetriever`). Exposed via the MCP server from `EPI
          +C-2.2`. **No new infra, no Neo4j, no separate vector DB.**

  INIT-6 at a glance

  7 epics built to deliver in this order:

  1. EPIC-6.1 Graph schema + Postgres spine_kg (pgvector) — foundation
  2. EPIC-6.2 Tree-sitter code parser (7 languages out of the box)
  3. EPIC-6.3 Document parser (markdown + Spine-ID resolution)
  4. EPIC-6.4 Incremental indexer (extends db/watcher/)
  5. EPIC-6.5 Query API + 8 MCP tools (find_callers, impact_radius, doc_for_region, …)
  6. EPIC-6.6 Role-prompt integration (6 roles teach themselves to use graph before grep)
  7. EPIC-6.7 Hybrid graph+vector RAG (LangChain)

  How LangChain/LangGraph landed (no new INIT — sprinkled where it earns)

  ┌────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            Backlog item            │                                   Tech role                                   │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EPIC-1.2 Swarm                     │ LangGraph subgraph for stateful fan-out/collect/synthesize with checkpointing │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EPIC-1.5 Router                    │ Borrow LangChain model-router primitives                                      │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EPIC-3.4 Evals (NEW)               │ LangSmith-style eval harness — closes the survey gap                          │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EPIC-6.5 Query tools               │ Wraps LangChain GraphRetriever                                                │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ EPIC-6.7 Hybrid RAG                │ LangChain MultiVectorRetriever + GraphRetriever + RRF                         │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Researcher / Datawright role tools │ LangChain agent-with-tools + pipelines (future stories under those roles)     │
  └────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The bash orchestration core stays untouched — that's the moat.
  
  Critical dependency you should know about

  INIT-6 has a real prerequisite relationship with INIT-1: STORY-1.3.3 (auto story-dependency detection) goes from
  heuristic → deterministic once EPIC-6.5 ships. So the right build order is:

  EPIC-1.7 (pipeline manifest)  ─┐
  EPIC-6.1 → 6.2 → 6.4           ├─→  EPIC-1.1 (intake)
  EPIC-2.2 (MCP server)          │    EPIC-1.2 (swarm — uses LangGraph)
  EPIC-6.5 (query tools)         ─┘    EPIC-1.3 (decomposer — uses graph)
                                       EPIC-6.6 (role integration)
                                       EPIC-6.7 (hybrid RAG)
                                       EPIC-3.4 (evals)

  The KG foundation (6.1–6.5) + the pipeline manifest (1.7) + the MCP scaffolding (2.2) are the first-sprint candidates —
  everything else gets cheaper to build once those land.

  Sign-off

  Two REQs now in Draft v1 awaiting your sign-off:
  - REQ-INIT-1-sdlc-front-door.md
  - REQ-INIT-6-knowledge-graph.md
  
  Want to review and approve, request changes, or push on a specific section? Same approval-gate flow we're literally
  designing in EPIC-1.4 applies — meta-recursion intact.

⏺ This is a substantial project — and you're right, there's massive overlap. The honest read:

  TRON in one paragraph

  TRON is an enterprise AI QA platform that runs a 7-layer verification pipeline (deterministic scanners → LLM ISO agents →
   schema-validated output → sandbox execution → cross-LLM validation → Platt-calibrated confidence → prompt-regression CI)
   to audit other people's code for security / quality / compliance findings that are verified, not hallucinated. Python +
  FastAPI + Postgres (pgvector + ltree + pgcrypto) + Temporal + Redis + MinIO + Vault + React. Six specialist "ISO agents" 
  run in parallel under an AuditManager (SecurityISO, BuilderISO, QAISO, PerformanceISO, ComplianceISO, DocumentationISO),
  each with declared scope (Blueprint), each with a deterministic pre-pass. Last touched 2026-05-02 — recent,
  well-developed (Version 5.4, "Implementation Ready").

  The overlap with Spine is enormous

  ┌────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐
  │     Capability     │              TRON has              │         Spine has          │            Notes            │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Multi-agent        │                                    │ 13 roles under             │ Same pattern. TRON's        │
  │ specialization     │ ISO agents under AuditManager      │ conductor/planner          │ "Blueprint scope" = Spine's │
  │                    │                                    │                            │  "role authority."          │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ BRD / TRD /        │ docs/project/BRD.md, TRD.md,       │ Just added as INIT-1       │ TRON already built this. We │
  │ Traceability docs  │ REQUIREMENTS_TRACEABILITY.md       │ artifacts                  │  could lift the schemas.    │
  │                    │                                    │ (PRD/TRD/Roadmap)          │                             │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Standards          │ Default (OWASP) → Company →        │ Org policy bundles         │ TRON already shipped the    │
  │ hierarchy          │ Project                            │ (INIT-2)                   │ exact 3-tier model Spine's  │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ INIT-2 is designing.        │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ MCP server         │ Implemented (tron/mcp/,            │ Planned (EPIC-2.2)         │ Lift the implementation.    │
  │                    │ requirements-mcp.txt)              │                            │                             │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Memory subsystem   │ tron/memory/                       │ per-role memory.md         │ Different shape; both       │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ exist.                      │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cost ledger        │ Per-audit cost surfaced in admin   │ costs.csv + make           │ Same idea.                  │
  │                    │ UI                                 │ team-budget                │                             │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │                    │                                    │                            │ TRON's ltree is exactly     │
  │ Postgres backbone  │ pgvector + ltree + pgcrypto        │ Plain Postgres (db/)       │ what Spine's                │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ INIT/EPIC/STORY hierarchy   │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ wants.                      │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Prompt regression  │ Shipped (tests/golden_suite/ +     │ Just added as EPIC-3.4     │ TRON already built the eval │
  │ CI                 │ GitHub Action)                     │                            │  harness. Lift it.          │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cross-LLM          │ Anthropic + OpenAI consensus check │ Not in plan                │ Real gap Spine doesn't      │
  │ validation         │                                    │                            │ address.                    │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Schema-enforced    │                                    │ Markdown-with-headers      │ TRON's typed contracts      │
  │ output             │ Pydantic FindingOutput validation  │ convention                 │ close the "fragile at       │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ scale" gap from the survey. │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sandbox execution  │                                    │                            │ Spine's engineer never      │
  │ verification       │ Docker ephemeral + seccomp         │ Not in plan                │ actually runs the code it   │
  │                    │                                    │                            │ ships. TRON does.           │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Confidence         │ Platt-scaled with sklearn          │ Nothing                    │ TRON has a real honesty     │
  │ calibration        │                                    │                            │ layer; Spine doesn't.       │
  ├────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
  │ Knowledge graph    │ None explicit                      │ Just designed (INIT-6)     │ Spine ahead here.           │
  └────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘

  The key difference (and the unifying insight)

  TRON audits code. Spine builds code. They're not duplicate efforts — they're two halves of the same problem:

          ┌───────────────┐       ┌───────────────┐
          │     SPINE     │  ──→  │     TRON      │
          │  (the BUILDER)│       │ (the VERIFIER)│
          │               │       │               │
          │ PRD → TRD →   │       │ 7-layer       │
          │ Roadmap →     │       │ verification: │
          │ Code (via     │       │ scanners +    │
          │ engineer)     │       │ ISOs + sandbox│
          │               │       │ + calibration │
          └───────────────┘       └───────────────┘
                builds                 trusts/blocks

  This is exactly what enterprise SDLC looks like: a team writes code, a separate function (QA / security / compliance)
  verifies it. TRON is the verification function Spine doesn't have built yet.

  What Spine should lift from TRON (concretely)

  Ranked by impact:
  
  1. Standards hierarchy (Default → Company → Project) — already shipped in TRON. Don't reinvent for Spine INIT-2; lift the
   model. Saves weeks.
  2. Pydantic-typed artifacts — make PRD / TRD / Roadmap / Report payloads Pydantic-validated. Closes
  "markdown-with-headers is fragile" gap. Borrow FindingOutput patterns.
  3. Postgres ltree for Spine's INIT/EPIC/STORY hierarchy + the knowledge graph (INIT-6). Native parent-child queries.
  4. Prompt regression harness — TRON's tests/golden_suite/ + GitHub Action is the implementation EPIC-3.4 was going to
  invent. Lift it.
  5. Sandbox execution verification — Spine's engineer ships untested claims; TRON's sandbox proves the code runs. Add as a
   new EPIC under INIT-3 (Trust & Reproducibility).
  6. Cross-LLM validation for high-stakes outputs — route PRD acceptance, TRD synthesis, security-critical engineer work
  through Anthropic + OpenAI consensus. New gap-closing capability.
  7. Confidence calibration on LLM-only outputs — Platt scaling for architect risk assessments, decomposer story estimates.
   Honesty layer Spine doesn't have.

  The strategic recommendation
  
  Position TRON as Spine's verification subsystem, not a separate competitor product. Concretely:

  - Spine's qa and auditor roles call TRON during the verification phase of every build.
  - TRON's ISO agents become specialist sub-agents Spine can dispatch (SecurityISO becomes a tool Spine's auditor uses).
  - TRON's 7-layer pipeline becomes the implementation of Spine's SDLC phase 7-8 (the verification phase in the REQ-INIT-1
  SDLC map).
  - TRON's Standards Hierarchy is the implementation of Spine's INIT-2 org policy bundles.
  - They share Postgres (TRON's already there; Spine extends it).
  - They share the MCP server.

  This gives both projects clean identity and lets you stop building two halves of the same thing in parallel.

  Big-picture observation
  
  You've been working on this idea for a while in two ways: TRON took the "verify everything, trust nothing" slice; Spine 
  is taking the "orchestrate everything from spec to ship" slice. Each is strong; combined they're a real product. The fact
   that TRON already shipped working versions of things Spine just put in the backlog (eval harness, standards hierarchy,
  MCP server, BRD/TRD schemas) means you can accelerate Spine significantly by lifting from TRON rather than rebuilding.

  What I'd do next

  Three options — let me know which (or all):

  1. Write docs/research/TRON_INTEGRATION.md — capture this analysis as a durable research artifact (like
  COMPETITIVE_LANDSCAPE.md).
  2. Add TRON-integration stories to the backlog — specifically: a new EPIC under INIT-2 (lift Standards Hierarchy), a new
  EPIC under INIT-3 (sandbox + calibration + cross-LLM validation), a story under EPIC-2.2 (lift MCP server from TRON), and
   a story under EPIC-3.4 (lift prompt-regression harness).
  3. Draft REQ-INIT-7-tron-integration.md — a dedicated REQ for the Spine↔TRON integration, since this is a meaningful new
  initiative (not just absorbing pieces).

  My recommendation: all three. This is too big to leave in chat memory. Want me to proceed, or do you want to look at TRON
   more deeply first before we commit?

⏺ Yes — and the moment you say "central orchestrator," the right shape becomes obvious. Let me cast it.
  
  The Plan / Build / Verify trifecta with a central orchestrator

                      ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                      │     SPINE ORCHESTRATOR              │
                      │                                     │
                      │  • Project lifecycle state machine  │
                      │  • Phase gates + user approvals     │

                      │    /Verify; reroute on failure      │
                      │  • Unified cost ledger + audit log  │
                      │  • Portfolio mgmt (many projects)   │
                      │  • Single user-facing UI / API      │
                      └────┬──────────┬───────────┬─────────┘
                           │          │           │
                    ┌──────▼──┐  ┌────▼─────┐  ┌──▼───────────┐
                    │  PLAN   │  │  BUILD   │  │   VERIFY     │
                    │         │  │          │  │              │
                    │ intake  │  │ engineer │  │ scanners +   │
                    │ PRD     │  │ operator │  │ ISO agents + │
                    │ TRD     │  │ datawright│ │ sandbox +    │
                    │ swarm   │  │ + roles  │  │ cross-LLM +  │
                    │ roadmap │  │ KG/MCP   │  │ calibration  │
                    └─────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘
                    (Spine    )  (Spine    )   (TRON, integrated)
                     INIT-1)      existing      as a subsystem)
                                   roles

                    ▲                ▲              ▲
                    └────────────────┼──────────────┘
                                     │
                     CROSS-CUTTING FOUNDATION
                     • Knowledge Graph (INIT-6)
                     • Standards/Policy (INIT-2 + TRON's hierarchy)
                     • MCP server (EPIC-2.2)
                     • Cost router (EPIC-1.5)
                     • Audit log (INIT-3)
                     • Memory & lessons

  Where the orchestrator actually lives

  It's the conductor evolved. Today Spine has a conductor role and a planner role doing some of this work. The new
  Orchestrator is what those become when Plan/Build/Verify are first-class phases with their own subsystems:

  - Not a new agent — a subsystem with its own state store (Postgres tables for projects, phases, gates, transitions)
  - Talks to Plan / Build / Verify via MCP tools (one consistent contract for all three)
  - Owns the lifecycle finite-state machine: intake → plan_in_progress → plan_approved → build_in_progress → build_complete
   → verify_in_progress → verify_approved → done (with failure transitions back)
  - Owns the user-facing surface (the dashboard / API the human interacts with). Plan/Build/Verify are invisible internals.

  The win: subsystems stay independently excellent. Spine Plan keeps its bash + markdown clarity; Spine Build keeps its
  role daemons; TRON keeps its sophisticated Python verification pipeline; orchestrator is a thin coordinator on top.

  What changes in the backlog

  Significant restructure (sketched, not committed yet):

  ┌───────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │               Today               │                                    Tomorrow                                    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INIT-1: SDLC Front Door           │ INIT-1: Plan Subsystem (same scope, narrower name)                             │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (Build is just "existing Spine    │ INIT-7: Build Subsystem (formalizes engineer/operator/datawright as a          │
  │ roles", no INIT)                  │ coordinated subsystem with KG/MCP integration)                                 │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (No verify)                       │ INIT-8: Verify Subsystem (TRON Integration) — lift TRON, wire as the           │
  │                                   │ verification subsystem                                                         │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (No orchestrator)                 │ INIT-9: Central Orchestrator — lifecycle state machine, routing, gates, UI     │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INIT-2 Enterprise Control         │ Stays — but absorbs TRON's Standards Hierarchy (already shipped) instead of    │
  │                                   │ reinventing                                                                    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INIT-3 Trust & Reproducibility    │ Stays — gains TRON's sandbox + calibration + cross-LLM as new epics            │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INIT-6 Knowledge Graph            │ Stays — cross-cutting foundation under all three pillars                       │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INIT-4 / INIT-5                   │ Stay as-is                                                                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  The PRD/TRD work we just did (REQ-INIT-1, REQ-INIT-6) doesn't get thrown out — it's exactly what Plan subsystem produces,
   now feeding into Build and Verify rather than dead-ending.

  Three big decisions you need to make

  Per our protocol I won't lock anything until you weigh in. The three are:

  1. Naming / branding — what's the umbrella product called?

  - Option A — "Spine" umbrella; TRON becomes "Spine Verify" (rebrand) or stays "TRON" as a module within Spine
  - Option B — New name entirely (Loom, Foundry, Helm, Atelier, etc.) — both Spine and TRON become subsystems under a new
  top-level brand
  - Option C — Keep both names, ship them as two products that interoperate via MCP/REST; document them as "Spine + TRON"
  stack

  My take: A. Spine is the active brand, you're invested in it, and "Plan / Build / Verify" naturally lives under one
  umbrella. TRON keeps its identity as the verify subsystem (good for the audit/security positioning that already exists).

  2. Orchestrator implementation — what runs the central state machine?

  - Option A — Bash + Postgres (consistent with Spine's debuggability moat)
  - Option B — Python + Temporal (consistent with TRON's existing durable-workflow infrastructure; TRON already has
  Temporal in production)
  - Option C — Bash facade + Postgres state, Python workers for heavy lifting (hybrid: orchestrator core in bash for
  debuggability; workers in Python where TRON-style sandboxing/sklearn/etc. are needed)

  My take: C. The orchestrator state machine + routing + lifecycle is trivial in bash + Postgres and stays debuggable.
  Heavy verification work (sandbox, calibration, cross-LLM) keeps its Python home. They communicate via MCP/REST. You don't
   have to pick a single language stack.
  
  3. Integration depth — how merged are Spine and TRON codebases?

  - Option A — Thin integration: Spine + TRON stay separate repos/services; orchestrator calls TRON via API/MCP. Fast to
  ship, lowest risk, TRON's production state unaffected.
  - Option B — Monorepo, separate deployables: one repo, but Spine bash + TRON Python coexist as separate runtimes that
  ship together.
  - Option C — Full merge: absorb TRON code into Spine repo; refactor for unified config/install/UX. Highest cost, most
  coherent.

  My take: A first, B later. Ship thin integration in 2-4 weeks; you get the unified flow working end-to-end without months
   of refactoring. After it proves out, consider monorepo. Skip full merge — TRON's done its own thing well; let it stay
  opinionated.
  
  What this means for the next sprint

  If you say yes to this direction, the work order shifts:

  1. First sprint: stand up the orchestrator skeleton (state machine + Postgres tables + minimal routing) + lift TRON's
  Standards Hierarchy into Spine INIT-2 (kills duplicate work fast).
  2. Second sprint: wire Plan → Build → Verify happy path. Plan = Spine intake (mock initially), Build = a single
  engineer-daemon execution, Verify = TRON audit run. End-to-end thread works even if each subsystem is bare-bones.
  3. Third sprint: start filling out the Plan subsystem (REQ-INIT-1 work) and the KG (INIT-6) which both subsystems benefit
   from.

  This sequencing buys you a working end-to-end skeleton fast, then thickens each subsystem in parallel.

  ---
  Sign-off

  2. Orchestrator stack? Bash+Postgres / Python+Temporal / Hybrid
  3. Integration depth? Thin via API / Monorepo / Full merge

  Once you pick, I'll:

  to "Plan"

  - Write REQ-INIT-8-tron-integration.md (how TRON wires in)
  - Save the unified-architecture decision to memory as the new product-level shape
  
  Want to lock those three answers, or push back / refine first?
