───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 Bash command

   git add build/runtime/build_dispatcher.py shared/mcp/tools/build.py orchestrator/bin/spine tools/smoke-test.sh && \
   git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
   feat(v2): real build_dispatch + build_completed — Spine produces brief,
   external implementer builds, Spine ingests + verifies

   The dogfood project (id=30, downloads-organizer) had a PRD draft but
   was about to hit the next wall at build_in_progress: build_dispatch
   was a stub. This closes that wall and adds the matching ingest path
   build_completed, mirroring the plan-side pattern from commit f072854.

   ## Architectural decision (worth flagging)

   The previous build_dispatch wrapped `build/bridge/v1_dispatcher.sh`
   (STORY-7.5.1 wave-7) — it shelled out to spawn v1 bash daemons in the
   same process tree. The v2 model is decoupled: Spine produces a
   structured Build Brief from the PRD; an external implementer (human,
   Claude Code, Cursor, …) builds out-of-band; the result comes back as a
   BuildArtifact via `spine build report`. This matches Spine's
   positioning ("enterprise-deployable virtual team for vibecoders under
   org control") better than process-tree daemon spawning.

   The bridge script at build/bridge/v1_dispatcher.sh is preserved on
   disk — can be re-wired as a build_dispatch backend later if a v1-style
   in-process build is needed. Just no longer the default path.

   ## What lands

   - build/runtime/build_dispatcher.py — dispatch_build() + ingest_build_
     artifact() + helpers. Mirrors plan/runtime/intake_runner.py exactly:
     same _psql / _merge_metadata / _write_audit shape, module-level
     constants for metadata keys + audit action names, errors carry stable
     `reason` codes, audit-write failures swallowed at the boundary (the
     metadata write is source of truth).

   - shared/mcp/tools/build.py — rewritten as thin MCP wrappers
     delegating to build.runtime.build_dispatcher. Both tools now real:
     - build_dispatch refuses without a validated PRD (reason=no_validated_
       prd), synthesizes a Build Brief from PRD goals+acceptance+scope+
       stakeholders+constraints, warns (doesn't fail) on missing TRD/
       Roadmap, persists to metadata.build_brief.
     - build_completed validates input via BuildArtifact.model_validate()
       (refuse-to-seal validator at schema level is the safety net),
       refuses on missing brief / project_id_mismatch / unknown EG-id
       ref, persists to metadata.build_artifact + appends to
       metadata.build_history[].

   - orchestrator/bin/spine — `spine build brief <id>` reads/pretty-prints
     metadata.build_brief; `spine build report <id> --artifact <path>`
     loads artifact JSON and calls build_completed via in-process
     transport (exit 2 if file missing, 3 if tool returned error).
  Briefing the agent.

  Smoke test: 73 PASS / 0 FAIL across 10 phases.

  Architectural pivot worth flagging

  The agent removed the v1 daemon bridge wiring from build_dispatch. The previous design (STORY-7.5.1) had build_dispatch
  shell out to build/bridge/v1_dispatcher.sh to spawn old bash daemons in the same process tree. The new design is
  decoupled: Spine produces the Build Brief, an external implementer (you, Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) does the actual
  coding out-of-band, then reports back via spine build report. This matches Spine's stated positioning
  ("enterprise-deployable virtual team for vibecoders") better. The bridge script is preserved on disk — re-wireable later
  if needed.

  Next sensible wedges (your call)

  1. Run verify_audit against project 30's BuildArtifact — TRON is real. Point it at the artifact (which is a stubbed
  BuildArtifact from smoke since no real code was built). Probably surfaces a few wiring bugs (D2-like issues — the verify
  tool exists but may not be hooked to the orchestrator's auto-trigger on build_complete → verify_in_progress).
  2. Fix the install.sh Python deps gap (D3/D4 from STATUS.md) — venv + pip install mcp pyyaml fastapi so a fresh clone
  works without manual setup.
  3. Actually do a real build — give the brief to Claude Code as a separate session, have it produce a real
  downloads-organizer CLI, ingest via spine build report, then run verify. This is the most fun but most expensive.

  What's the move?

✻ Cooked for 1m 56s

❯ what do you recommand                                                                                                    

⏺ #1 first, then decide. Run verify_audit against project 30's existing stub artifact — it's a 5-minute test that exercises
   the last untested edge (orchestrator → TRON), and we'll know in one run whether the verify pipeline is genuinely wired
  or has another stub-class bug hiding. If it works cleanly, the loop is proven end-to-end and #3 (real build) becomes
  "produce a useful product" rather than "test the system." If it breaks, we have a precise next bug to fix.

  The tradeoff: #1 is faster and lower-risk; you might want #3 anyway just to actually have downloads-organizer exist as a
  real CLI, but doing #1 first means #3 isn't gated on discovering another wiring bug mid-build.

⏺ Dogfood probe #1: verify_audit result
     
  Tool is wired correctly. Stops at the deployment boundary.

  ┌───────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Layer       │                                             Result                                             │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tool registered   │ ✅                                                                                             │
  │ Tool registered   │ ✅                                                                                             │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Schema validates  │ ✅ — caught 4 separate field mismatches in my hand-crafted artifact (change_type,              │
  │ input             │ impact_distance, tokens_input shape, duration_seconds/delta consistency). Sharp schema         │
  │                   │ discipline.                                                                                    │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Refuses draft     │ ✅ (per EPIC-7.4 — won't burn LLM dollars on unsealed)                                         │
  │ artifacts         │                                                                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sealed artifact   │ ✅                                                                                             │
  │ accepted          │                                                                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Calls TRON's      │ ❌ — verify.tron.agents.manager.AuditManager not importable: No module named 'tron'            │
  │ AuditManager      │                                                                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Error path        │ ✅ — structured error.code="tron_not_available", retryable=False, message points at            │
  │                   │ STORY-8.2.x and the fix                                                                        │
  └───────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Why TRON isn't importable

  Two layers:
  1. PYTHONPATH — TRON code lives at verify/tron/ but uses absolute tron.foo.bar imports internally, so it needs verify/
  (not the repo root) on PYTHONPATH. Currently neither the orchestrator nor install.sh adds it.
  2. Deps — verify/requirements.txt has 84 lines: sqlalchemy[asyncio], asyncpg, pgvector, temporalio, redis, openai,
  anthropic, minio, etc. Even the bare import tron.agents.manager fails at line 8 of tron/infra/llm/budget.py on from
  sqlalchemy import .... Standalone TRON has its own env (per verify/SUBSYSTEM_BOUNDARY.md G-8 "standalone deployability").
   The unified Spine install has none of it.

  What this means

  The orchestrator → verify wiring is correct. What's missing is the deployment integration — bringing TRON's runtime
  online inside the Spine install. That's not 1-line glue; it's:

  - Adding verify/ to PYTHONPATH (1 line)
  - pip install -r verify/requirements.txt (~80 deps, ~hundreds of MB, ~few min)
  - Spinning up Temporal + Redis + MinIO (per TRON's docker-compose)
  - Configuring OpenAI + Anthropic API keys
  - Maybe wiring TRON's own Postgres schema (it has alembic migrations that need running, separate from Spine's flyway)

  Three honest options

  1. Stop the dogfood here — we've now proven every layer from spine intake through build_completed to the doorstep of
  TRON. The next layer is deployment, not code. Document the gap, move on.
  2. Build a "scanner-only" verify mode — TRON's deterministic scanners (Bandit/Semgrep/ESLint/OSV) don't need LLMs or
  Temporal. Add a verify_audit_lite (or a mode=scanner_only flag) that wraps just those, skips ISO agents + sandbox +
  cross-LLM. Useful product for many users, dogfoodable today, ~1 agent.
  3. Install TRON deps + wire it up — biggest, but proves the full vision. ~half-day with all the API key + Temporal setup.

  Recommend #2 — it's the same shape of work as the last 3 agents (wire a real implementation behind an MCP tool), and a
  scanner-only verify is genuinely useful for orgs that want fast/cheap (and offline) verification. #3 can come later when
  you've decided on the deployment story. #1 is the cop-out.

  What's the move?

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 Bash command
  
   git add orchestrator/bin/spine tools/smoke-test.sh tools/_smoke_phase11_tron.py tools/_tron_alembic_upgrade.py
   tools/_tron_dogfood_probe.py verify/.env verify/docker-compose.override.yml verify/SUBSYSTEM_BOUNDARY.md && \
   git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
   feat(v2): TRON live inside Spine — verify pipeline runnable end-to-end

   The dogfood probe last night stopped at `tron_not_available: No module
   named 'tron'`. This lands the minimum viable TRON deployment so the
   orchestrator → verify_audit → AuditManager path actually runs.

   TRON is the existing standalone product subtree-merged at verify/
   (commit e8687bc). The integration is additive — TRON's standalone
   deployability (REQ-INIT-8 G-8) is preserved.

   ## Service subset — postgres only

   AuditManager.__init__ doesn't hit DB/Redis/Temporal — those are async
   paths that no-op when _session_factory is None. LLM calls go via
   httpx (no Anthropic/OpenAI SDK indirection). Bandit/Semgrep run via
   LocalSandbox subprocess (no Docker-in-Docker). One postgres container
   is enough for the in-process integration. Skipped: pgbouncer, redis,
   minio, temporal, temporal-ui, tron-api, tron-worker, tron-sandbox,
   nginx, prometheus, grafana, tempo, loki, otel-collector, alertmanager,
   tron-backup.

   ## Port + container hygiene

   Spine's postgres untouched at 127.0.0.1:33001. TRON's postgres mapped
   to 127.0.0.1:33010 via verify/docker-compose.override.yml. Container
   renamed to spine_tron_postgres so it can coexist with a standalone
   TRON install (tron-postgres) if the user has one.

   ## Deps

   5 new pip packages requested → 12 transitive into .venv:
     sqlalchemy[asyncio]>=2.0.36 temporalio bandit alembic asyncpg psycopg2-binary
     + greenlet protobuf types-protobuf mako markupsafe stevedore
     + pygments markdown-it-py mdurl rich

   .venv: 64 MiB → 149 MiB (+85 MiB). None of the 84 lines in
   verify/requirements.txt were blanket-installed — only what AuditManager
   actually pulls in.

   Note: verify/requirements.txt pins sqlalchemy==2.0.23 which breaks
   on Python 3.14 (AssertionError on SQLCoreOperations). Worked around
   in our .venv install (>=2.0.36); TRON-side fix needed to update the
   pin (documented as TRON issue, not patched from Spine).

   ## PYTHONPATH wiring

   orchestrator/bin/spine:_mcp_inprocess_call — heredoc now puts
   SPINE_HOME first (for shared.*, plan.*, build.*) and verify/ second
   so TRON's absolute `tron.*` imports resolve. The wrapper's
   `verify.tron.*` style also works via PEP 420 implicit-namespace
   packages (verify/ has no __init__.py — that's by design).

   ## API key strategy — no magic

   Wrapper's existing tron_keys_missing error path is correct. Smoke
   test doesn't need keys. Probe runs without user input. With valid
   keys present, the dispatch path runs both deterministic_scanners +
   iso_swarm layers. If keys are stale/invalid, agent exceptions are
   swallowed by AuditManager._dispatch_agents and verify returns

⏺ TRON is live. Verify pipeline runnable end-to-end.

  Committed as 493b07c. Smoke: 78 PASS / 0 FAIL across 11 phases.

  What this actually means
  
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┐
  │                             Layer                             │        Before         │           After           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ verify_audit MCP tool                                         │ Real, but stopped at  │ Real, calls into live     │
  │                                                               │ tron_not_available    │ AuditManager              │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ TRON import                                                   │ No module named       │ Imports clean (PYTHONPATH │
  │                                                               │ 'tron'                │  wired)                   │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ TRON's 6 ISO agents                                           │ Inaccessible          │ Register on init          │
  │ (Security/Builder/QA/Performance/Compliance/Documentation)    │                       │                           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ Deterministic scanners (Bandit/Semgrep)                       │ Not invokable         │ Run via LocalSandbox      │
  │                                                               │                       │ subprocess                │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │                                                               │                       │ spine_tron_postgres on    │
  │ TRON's own Postgres                                           │ Not running           │ 33010, alembic head=008,  │
  │                                                               │                       │ 14 tables                 │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘

  Smart minimization

  Agent kept it tight: 1 container, not 15. AuditManager doesn't need Redis/Temporal/MinIO for in-process use — those are
  async paths that no-op cleanly. Saved gigabytes of images and hundreds of MB of RAM.

  Three TRON-side issues surfaced but not patched

  Per the "don't touch TRON code" guardrail:
  1. SQLAlchemy 2.0.23 pin broken on Python 3.14 — worked around in .venv with >=2.0.36; TRON's requirements.txt needs a
  bump
  2. Bandit/Semgrep findings suppressed when LLM unavailable — TRON treats deterministic tool output as LLM context only.
  Degraded-mode emit-tool-findings-only would be a useful TRON feature
  3. tron-worker healthcheck assumes REDIS_PASSWORD set — only matters if you bring up that service
  
  All three documented in verify/SUBSYSTEM_BOUNDARY.md.

  Full stub picture

  24 of 27 MCP tools real. Remaining stubs: graph_query, iso_invoke, org_standards_get — all off the critical path.

  The complete dogfood pipeline now exists
  
  spine project new          → real DB write
  spine intake               → 12-question template, draft PRD
  spine project approve      → HMAC-signed phase advance
  spine build brief          → Build Brief derived from PRD
  spine build report         → ingest BuildArtifact, audit
  verify_audit (MCP)         → TRON AuditManager + 6 ISO agents + scanners

  End-to-end, real data, real audit trail. The downloads-organizer project (id=30) has every layer's output sitting in
  project.metadata jsonb.

  What's left

  The whole pipeline is runnable. What's missing for a fully usable product is mostly polish:
  - Install.sh setup gap (D3/D4) — the dep install + venv + TRON service bring-up is currently 5-7 manual commands. Belongs
   in a make bootstrap or install.sh target
  - The 3 remaining stubs — non-critical but visible
  - Spine doctor should also check TRON — currently only reports MCP + Postgres
  - A real-code dogfood — give the brief to Claude Code, actually build downloads-organizer, ingest, run verify against
  real code (would surface real findings with real API keys)

  What's the next move?

   feat(v2): make bootstrap — one-command cold start, doctor refresh,
   F2 flyway sync closed

   Before this commit a fresh clone needed ~7 manual commands across two
   databases, two migration tools, a Python venv, and ad-hoc pip
   installs. Documented in scattered places. The wave-9 F2 follow-up was
   still unaddressed (manual psql apply of V2 + V14-V21 left Flyway
   history out of sync; `docker compose up watcher` needed --no-deps).

   After this commit:

     git clone <spine> && cd spine
     make bootstrap        # ~3-5 min cold
     make doctor           # 9 sections of structured checks
     bash tools/smoke-test.sh   # 84 PASS / 0 FAIL

   ## What lands

   - Top-level Makefile: bootstrap, bootstrap-clean, doctor, nuke,
     flyway-sync, smoke targets. All pre-existing team-* / lint-* / db-*
     targets preserved verbatim. `make help` is auto-generated from `##`
     doc comments.

   - tools/bootstrap.sh (~200 lines): 8 idempotent steps — preflight
     binaries → venv → pip install -r requirements.txt → spine pg up →
     tron pg up → flyway-sync (incl. F2 fix) → tron alembic → smoke
     acceptance gate. SKIP_SMOKE=1 to bypass the gate. Each step
     short-circuits when satisfied so re-runs are fast.

   - tools/spine-flyway-sync.sh (~190 lines): the F2 fix as a
     reproducible helper. Inserts flyway_schema_history rows for V2 +
     V14-V21 with checksum=0 then runs `flyway repair` to compute the
     real checksums. Verified: V14 now Success, V21 Success, V2
     Out-of-Order-but-applied (acceptable). `docker compose up watcher`
     no longer needs --no-deps.

   - requirements.txt (top-level, new, ~45 deps): the actual runtime pip
     set pinned to what .venv has. Excludes the 84-dep
     verify/requirements.txt — only the TRON subset the in-process
     AuditManager needs (sqlalchemy[asyncio]==2.0.49, asyncpg,
     temporalio, bandit, alembic).

   - orchestrator/bin/spine cmd_doctor: 3-line check → 9-section structured
     report. Probes host binaries, .venv + critical imports + tool count,
     Spine pg, Spine schemas, TRON pg, TRON in-process AuditManager
     constructable, MCP transport, bundle config, LLM keys (informational).
     --verbose adds remediation hints. Exit codes 0 / 1 / 4.

⏺ Fair critique — I was over-sequential. Honest reasons:

  Genuinely sequential (couldn't parallelize): plan_dispatch → build_dispatch → TRON → real-build → verify all gate on each
   other; the bug fixes were discovered mid-flight (audit pipeline, FastMCP API drift) and blocked next steps; the 5
  sandbox retries on the build agent depended on the prior failure's diagnostic.
  
  Was sequential by choice but didn't need to be: bootstrap + doctor refresh + flyway-sync could've been three agents
  instead of one. The build agent + a separate "polish synthesize_prd_draft + fix the CLI flag bug + clean up the synth
  tier-splitting bug" agent could've run in parallel. I defaulted to one-at-a-time because dogfood loops are easier to
  debug serially — but you're right that's leaving leverage on the table.                               

  Parallelizable right now while verify_audit runs:

  ┌───────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Agent │                                              Independent work                                               │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │       │ Fix Spine bugs surfaced today: synthesize_prd_draft accepts substanceless answers + splits                  │
  │ A     │ MUST/SHOULD/COULD by . + build_dispatch UUID-vs-BIGINT inconsistency + the spine build report --artifact    │
  │       │ flag parse bug + build_dispatch SPINE_DB_URL env requirement                                                │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ B     │ Implement the 3 remaining MCP stubs (graph_query, iso_invoke, org_standards_get) — all wrap real backend    │
  │       │ code                                                                                                        │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C     │ Update spine doctor + bootstrap to install pytest (it wasn't in requirements.txt); also document the        │
  │       │ additionalDirectories propagation gap so future dogfooders don't hit the 5-attempt sandbox loop             │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ D     │ Real-code findings agent — runs after verify_audit returns, files what TRON found as backlog items          │
  └───────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  A/B/C are independent; D depends on verify_audit. Want me to fire A+B+C in parallel now while verify_audit finishes?

⏺ verify_audit completed — but with an important finding worth flagging now while the parallel agents run:

  verify_audit result on real downloads-organizer code

  status: ok
  pass_fail: pass
  layers_run: [deterministic_scanners, iso_swarm]
  findings: []
  duration: 8s

  Surface read: "Pass, no findings, ship it." Honest read: TRON ran but had nothing to scan. Two reasons:

  1. The API key 401'd (LLM call failed after 3 attempts). The deterministic scanners (Bandit/Semgrep) ran but TRON's
  design only emits findings via the LLM ISO agents — degraded mode swallows the deterministic output (the TRON-side bug we
   already documented in verify/SUBSYSTEM_BOUNDARY.md).
  2. TRON had no source files to read. The BuildArtifact has diff_hash (sha256) but no diff_text / source_url / file
  content. So even with a working LLM, the ISO agents only had metadata to look at — names + line counts, no actual code.

  This is a real architectural finding: the Spine → external-implementer → verify loop is data-shape-incomplete. To make
  verify actually work on dogfood code, BuildArtifact needs one of: code_changes[].diff_text, code_changes[].source_url
  (file:// or git://), or an out-of-band attachment mechanism. The closed loop runs, but the verification step is a no-op
  because TRON can't see what's outside Spine's repo.

  I'll fold this into Agent D's brief when it runs. Three parallel agents (A: bugs, B: stubs, C: pytest+docs) still in
  flight.

✻ Cooked for 32s · 3 local agents still running

⏺ Agent "Drift audit: 2 days of Spine work" completed

⏺ The drift audit is back — and it's important. Honest distillation for you:

  Three findings that matter

  1. The dogfood loop is ceremonially closed, not actually closed

  verify_audit returned pass_fail=pass, findings=[], which I reported earlier. The auditor caught that this is structurally
   vacuous — TRON read zero source files. Reason: shared/mcp/tools/verify.py:199-214 _load_file_contents() resolves
  Path(ch.path) against Spine's cwd (SpineDevelopment/), not the implementer's repo (~/Projects/downloads-organizer/).
  Combined with the LLM 401, the "pass" is just "we had nothing to look at." This is the most material correctness gap in
  the whole 2 days of work. Smallest fix: record implementer repo root on the project at build_completed time + rebase
  relative paths in _load_file_contents. ~30 LOC, 1 commit.                                   

  2. PRD FR-5 (MCP-only) silently violated in 4 places                                             

  build/runtime/build_dispatcher.py:37 imports from plan.artifacts.prd_v1; shared/memory/lesson_store.py:20 imports from
  build.kg.embeddings; shared/schemas/build/build_artifact.py:18 imports from plan.artifacts._base;
  orchestrator/bin/spine:279 runs from plan.runtime.intake_runner import run_intake directly via heredoc — despite the
  CLI's own docstring saying "Subsystems are reached only through MCP." Either move shared schemas to shared/schemas/ +
  rewrite CLI's intake dispatch to use the in-process MCP transport (a few hours), OR explicitly amend PRD FR-5 to admit
  "schema imports OK; runtime behavior through MCP." Your call which posture is correct.      

  3. 5-move intake protocol exists only in the role prompt, not the runtime

  This is the deepest drift relative to your stated memory. plan/runtime/intake_runner.py is a deterministic YAML-question
  loop — naive cast / provoke / reframe / tier / artifact appear zero times in the runtime; only in
  lib/role-prompts/product.md. Today's "MUST: x" bug surfaced exactly this — Spine had no provoke step to push back on the
  thin answer. Agent A's fix (substance heuristic) papers over it; the real fix is wiring the conductor + product role to
  actually drive the dialogue via LLM, with the question template as scaffolding rather than the whole game.

  Plus three smaller-but-real

  - BACKLOG.md is stale: 4 P0 stories marked Backlog actually shipped; STATUS.md count is wrong by 17 (66 stories remain,
  not 49); 28 P0 stories still in backlog
  - verify/docker-compose.override.yml breaks TRON's standalone-deployability promise (G-8) — file is inside verify/ so cd 
  verify && docker compose up picks it up. Move the override to the repo root
  - In-process Python MCP transport (the one that actually works) was added in commit 1a2fb30 but isn't in PRD or BACKLOG.
  Drift-by-pragmatism. Either bless it in PRD §FR-5 or replace with real Streamable-HTTP

  What we got right (preserve these)
  
  Bash orchestration core intact; LangChain/LangGraph correctly optional with linear fallback; BuildArtifact schema
  validators caught real bugs; TRON subtree boundary clean; MCP envelope validation actually validates; bootstrap surface
  is real product improvement.
  
  Overall verdict: yellow, trending red on contracts. Cheap to fix now, expensive in 2 weeks.

  Agent A (5 bugs) and hygiene auditor still running. Want me to also dispatch a #4 to fix the verify path-resolution bug
  now (it's small enough — ~30 LOC), or wait until you've digested?

✻ Baked for 16s · 2 local agents still running

⏺ Agent "Workspace hygiene audit" completed

⏺ Hygiene audit back. The headline finding is worse than I expected — and there's a real Spine design proposal at the end.
  Distilling:

  Today's mess (5 biggest)

  1. db/ is a 1.1 MB untracked subtree — production-load-bearing migrations V1–V13, the dashboard, docker-compose, watcher
  service — all referenced by Makefile.v2 but not in git. This is the biggest "where did it go" risk in the repo. db/.env
  is gitignored (sensitive); the rest needs committing.
  2. 149 empty /tmp/spine-<role>-NN/ dirs — pre-allocated 4 days ago, never touched. Pre-allocating worker slots is an
  anti-pattern.
  3. Orphan MCP PID + log at /tmp/spine-mcp.{pid,log} — PID 49718 is dead; the file will false-positive "already running"
  on next start.
  4. 26 wave-9 files untracked in lib/ — 12 production scripts + 13 test scripts + lib/spine-migrate.py. Until committed,
  every git status is unreadable.
  5. tools/_*.py underscore convention is undocumented — 4 files; a new contributor will assume "underscore = dead" and
  delete them. One tools/README.md fixes this.

  Plus .gitignore gaps: missing .ruff_cache/, recursive **/.DS_Store, .pytest_cache/, *.env.bak.

  Spine's design answer (proposed 6 stories)

  The auditor sketched a clean design anchored in what Spine already has:
  
  - Workspace convention: <repo>/.spine/work/<phase>/<role>/<run_id>/ — never /tmp, never pre-allocated. Subdirs: in/,
  out/, log/, state/. (STORY-9.1.1)
  - Promotion contract: artifacts leave workspace only via MCP — db_insert, git_commit, or canonical_copy. Agent never 
  promotes; orchestrator does in response to mcp.promote(...). One chokepoint for policy + one audit entry per promotion.
  (STORY-9.1.4)
  - Janitor service: subscribes to phase-transition events; tar-gzips workspaces under .spine/archive/<run_id>.tgz, deletes
   live tree; daily sweep shovels archives >7d to Postgres (or S3). (STORY-9.1.2)
  - make hygiene / spine hygiene: surfaces today's bucketing (safe-to-delete / promote / keep-but-justify), supports --fix,
   driven by shared/standards/hygiene.yaml. (STORY-9.1.3)
  - Conductor refuses phase-done if spine hygiene check non-zero for that run's workspace. CI runs make hygiene-check.
  Opt-in pre-commit hook blocks __pycache__, *.env, .spine/work/ files. (STORY-9.1.5)
  - Migrate the underscore mess: rename tools/_*.py per a documented convention or add tools/README.md. (STORY-9.1.6 — XS)

  Where we are

  - Agent A (5 bug fixes) — still running
  - Agents B (3 stubs), C (pytest + sandbox doc), Drift auditor, Hygiene auditor — all DONE, uncommitted
  - The verify-audit-vacuous finding from drift + the db/-untracked finding from hygiene + the PRD FR-5 violations are all
  real and stack up

  Once A reports, I'll commit B + C with crisp messages. Drift + hygiene findings I'll roll into a STATUS.md §9 (after I
  see what you want to do — fix urgently, file as stories, both?).

  What's your call on the three big findings? (a) Fix verify path-resolution now, (b) commit db/, (c) file the 6 hygiene
  stories into BACKLOG?

  ---
  Question 2: The drift audit's biggest finding was: the dogfood loop is ceremonially closed, not actually closed.
  verify_audit returned pass_fail=pass, findings=[] — but TRON read zero source files because _load_file_contents()
  resolves relative paths against Spine's cwd, not the implementer's repo. The "pass" is just "we had nothing to look at."
  
  The fix is small (~30 LOC): record implementer repo root in project.metadata.build_root at build_completed time, then
  rebase Path(ch.path) in verify.py:199-214 against it.

  The real choice — is this what we actually want? Two postures:

  - Posture A — "Spine stores everything inline": extend BuildArtifact's CodeChange to carry diff_text (or full source)
  inline. Spine becomes self-contained — doesn't need to reach out to the implementer's filesystem. More robust (artifact
  is portable, archivable, replayable), heavier (artifacts get big), schema change ripples through audit + storage. Treats
  the artifact as the truth.
  - Posture B — "Spine knows where the code lives": just store build_root (a path or git URL) on the project, and Spine
  reads from there at verify time. Lighter, smaller change. But couples verify to the implementer's filesystem being intact
   + reachable + at the right state.

  Which posture matches your intent for Spine?

✻ Sautéed for 48s

❯ since you offered two choices, there must be a reason you offered two choices for each of their benefits.... why two     
  choices?                                                                                                                 
  
⏺ Fair call. The honest answer:
  
  I gave two choices because they map to two different visions of what Spine fundamentally is — and I wasn't sure which one
   matches your intent. Let me actually take a position now.

  The underlying tension:

  - Posture A (inline content) treats Spine as a system of record. The artifact IS the truth — verify can replay against
  the artifact's content alone, six months from now, with the implementer's laptop long gone. This matches three things I
  read in your memory: "recording" is explicit in the tech stack (Postgres + pgvector for recording + KG),
  shared/reproducibility/ (RunManifest + replay + diff) presupposes self-contained inputs, and "enterprise-deployable"
  implies compliance auditors who want the trail to be intact independent of any one machine.
  - Posture B (path reference) treats Spine as a coordinator. The git repo is the truth; Spine just points at where to
  look. Lighter, matches the "thin orchestrator" ethos. But it kills replay the moment the implementer's repo moves / is
  deleted / drifts.

  Reading the memory: Spine wants to be a system of record. "Recording" is in the tech stack memo. Replay is in
  shared/reproducibility/. Compliance is in the positioning memo. So pure B isn't right.

  But pure A is heavy — every CodeChange carrying full source bloats the DB fast.

  My actual recommendation: a hybrid that respects both. Store build_root on the project AND content_sha256 on each
  CodeChange (already there) AND archive the actual file content to a content-addressed blob store (.spine/blobs/<sha256>
  or eventually S3) at build_completed time. Verify reads from the blob store, not the implementer's filesystem. The
  artifact carries hashes, not bytes — but the bytes are recoverable from the blob store as long as Spine has been keeping
  its promises.

  This way: schema stays light (hashes only); replay works (blobs are immutable + addressable); the implementer's
  filesystem can move/disappear; compliance gets a real audit trail.

  Why I held back from leading with this: it's a bigger change than the original "30 LOC path-resolution fix" the drift
  audit proposed. It introduces a blob store, a promotion hook, and a verify-side reader. Probably ~150-200 LOC across
  build_dispatcher.py, verify.py, and a new shared/blobs/store.py. That's still small for the value, but it deserved an
  explicit ask rather than me unilaterally upgrading scope.

  So the real question is: do you want the 30 LOC patch (option B — pragmatic, restores honesty to the dogfood loop today)
  or the hybrid blob store (180 LOC — restores honesty AND makes verify replayable, which is what Spine's principles
  already say it should be)?

⏺ Apologies — I jumped from the actual problem to inventing a "blob store" without justification. Let me back up and use
  plain words.

  The actual problem (one sentence): when Spine ran verify_audit, it didn't know where on disk the implementer's code
  lived, so TRON read no source files and the "pass" was empty.

  What I should have said: The implementer's code lives in a git repo (the build agent did git init + commit; we have hash
  f181195). Three plain options for telling Spine where to look:

  ┌────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Option   │                         What Spine stores                         │     How verify gets the code      │
  ├────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (a) Local  │ A filesystem path: ~/Projects/downloads-organizer/                │ Spine reads the files directly    │
  │ path       │                                                                   │ from disk at verify time          │
  ├────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (b) Git    │ A repo URL + commit SHA (~/Projects/downloads-organizer @ f181195 │ Spine git fetch/git show at       │
  │ ref        │  or git@github.com:... @ <sha>)                                   │ verify time                       │
  ├────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ (c) Inline │ The actual file contents stuffed inside the BuildArtifact         │ Spine reads from its own DB; no   │
  │            │                                                                   │ external dependency               │
  └────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

  My recommendation: (b) git ref. Reasons in plain language:
  - Engineers already use git; nothing new to learn
  - Git already does the hard parts: immutable hashes, archival, transferability
  - When you mentioned "github" you were pointing at exactly this — Spine should know which commit was built, not just "a
  path on someone's laptop"
  - A path (option a) breaks the moment the laptop is gone or the files moved — kills replay
  - Inline (option c) bloats the DB and duplicates what git already stores
  
  Cost of (b): add two fields to BuildArtifact — source_repo (URL or path) and source_commit_sha. Verify resolves files via
   git -C <source_repo> show <sha>:<path> instead of open(<path>). ~50-80 LOC across build_artifact.py and verify.py. No
  new infra, no blob store, no schema gymnastics.

  That's the proposal in plain English. The "blob store" I mentioned was me inventing scope. Forget it.

  Does (b) match what you had in mind when you said "github"?

⏺ OK let me start over in plain English and actually think this through.
  
  The problem (in one paragraph)

  When the implementer built downloads-organizer on disk and sent Spine a "here's what I built" report, the report listed
  file paths like src/downloads_organizer/cli.py. Spine's verify step tried to read those files, but it didn't know which 
  directory those paths are relative to. It looked in its own folder, didn't find them, and silently scanned nothing. The
  "pass" result was just "we found nothing to look at" — not "we looked carefully and the code is clean."

  What you actually want (reading your message back)

  ▎ "Spine runs, does its thing, and creates the software... It can maintain its own authority meanwhile also creating 
  ▎ necessary historical and proper sourcing for sharing and incidentals like laptop loss."

  I read this as two things at once:

  1. Spine is the source of truth. When something is built and reported into Spine, Spine should HAVE that thing — not just
   a pointer to where it lives on someone's laptop. If the laptop dies, gets reformatted, or the implementer's git repo
  gets force-pushed, Spine should still be able to show the auditor exactly what was built and verified.
  2. Spine remembers the provenance. It should also know "this came from ~/Projects/downloads-organizer/ at commit f181195
  built by khash@khash.com on May 17" — because humans want to trace things back, and engineers want to go look at the
  original.

  These aren't competing — they're complementary. Spine owns the artifact AND remembers where it came from.

  The proposal (one design)

  When the implementer calls spine build report, Spine does three things, not two:
  
  1. Validates the artifact (already does this)
  2. Captures the actual code — Spine reaches into the source location, reads the files referenced in the artifact, and
  stores those bytes inside Spine itself (in Postgres, content-addressed by sha256 — so duplicate files don't re-store)
  3. Records the provenance — Spine writes source_repo (the git URL or path), source_commit_sha, built_by alongside the
  artifact metadata

  After this, Spine has everything it needs to verify, replay, or audit without ever needing to touch the implementer's
  filesystem again. The verify step reads from Spine's own captured copy. Six months later, a compliance auditor can do
  spine show artifact <id> and get the actual code that was reviewed.
  
  If the implementer's repo is later force-pushed or deleted, Spine doesn't care — it has its copy. The provenance fields
  tell future-you where it came from.

  Why this matches your stated principles

  - "Spine maintains its own authority" → Spine's storage IS the truth; verify doesn't reach outside
  - "Historical and proper sourcing" → provenance (repo + commit + builder) is recorded
  - "Sharing" → the artifact is self-contained; you can hand someone a Spine project export and they get everything
  - "Incidentals like laptop loss" → Spine has its own copy; laptop irrelevance

  What I need from you before going further

  This is a real design choice that ripples through BuildArtifact schema, build_completed, verify_audit, and the
  audit/replay paths. Before I dispatch anyone to implement, I want to confirm I've understood you correctly:

  Is "Spine captures the code into its own storage at build-report time, AND records provenance" the right design in your 
  head? If yes, I'll think about how to break it into one (or two) commits and come back with that. If no, tell me what I
  got wrong.

⏺ Yes — now I get it. And my earlier "Spine captures the bytes" proposal was actually wrong for what you're describing. Let
   me play your scenario back so we're synced:

  Your scenario, restated

  Day 1: You vibecode "project widget" through Spine — intake, PRD, TRD, brief, build (lives on GitHub), verify, ship.

  Day 14: You come back. "Spine, I want to add login to widget."
  
  Spine:
  1. Already knows widget — loads the PRD, TRD, prior decisions, history
  2. Runs intake again — but constrained by what's already there ("we have these auth surfaces; what does login mean —
  OAuth? username/password? SSO?")
  3. Produces an enhancement brief
  4. The brief tells the implementer "modify these 3 functions; here's who calls them; here's what they depend on" —
  derived from Spine's Knowledge Graph of widget's codebase
  5. Implementer (you, a Claude Code session, whoever) builds it, commits to GitHub
  6. Spine ingests, re-indexes KG against the new commit, verifies, ships 
  
  The crucial design implication I had wrong:

  GitHub owns the bytes. Spine owns the knowledge layer on top:
  - PRD/TRD/Brief/audit/history (persistent project memory)
  - Knowledge Graph (the map: functions, calls, files, dependencies, who-owns-what)
  - Provenance (which GitHub URL, which commit_sha, who built it, when)
  
  When Spine needs code, it fetches from GitHub at the recorded commit — not from its own copy. And the KG lets the AI
  agents be surgical: instead of reading 50k LOC to find where login should hook in, they query the graph for
  find_callers("authenticate") and get 4 functions in 200ms.

  I was proposing Spine duplicate GitHub's job (store bytes). That's wrong. GitHub already does archival, versioning, and
  incidentals (force-push protection, hosting, etc.). Spine's authority is over the understanding of the code, not the
  bytes.
  
  What this means for the dogfood fix + going forward

  The actual fix for the dogfood loop is smaller than "blob store" and aligned with this design:

  1. BuildArtifact carries: source_repo (GitHub URL or local git path), source_commit_sha. These are the provenance — Spine
   remembers where the code lives.
  2. verify_audit fetches code via git: git -C <source_repo> show <commit_sha>:<path> instead of open(path). Works against
  any git ref — local, GitHub, GitLab, whatever. Files come from git, not from disk wandering.
  3. build_completed triggers a KG re-index of the touched files at the recorded commit. So the graph stays in sync. This
  is the piece that enables the Day-14 scenario — when you come back, the KG reflects the real codebase.
  4. spine project resume <project> or spine project enhance <project> (new CLI command): re-engages with an existing
  project. Loads PRD/TRD/Brief/audit from Spine's DB, runs intake constrained by prior context, dispatches with KG-aware
  brief.

  The first three are concrete, small changes. The fourth is the bigger feature that fulfills your Day-14 scenario.

  Did I get it right?
  
  Specifically: GitHub = bytes; Spine = knowledge + provenance + KG; Spine never duplicates GitHub's job; KG is what makes 
  AI agents token-efficient. Yes/no?

  If yes — I'll think about how to break this into commits (probably: provenance fields first, then git-fetch in verify,
  then KG re-index hook, then the resume/enhance command). Won't start until you confirm.

❯ yes.. better... one more thing.. in a true SDLC, we have different things like BUGS, FEATURES etc.. that come back to    
  the code... we need to be able to handle them all.. spine should be able to do handle them all.. Dont forget SPINE is

⏺ Got it. Let me play this back to make sure I have it right, because this is a meaningful expansion.
  
  What "Spine is the team" actually means

  Today's Spine model treats one "project" as one unit of work that flows: intake → plan → build → verify → ship. That
  model works for building project widget on Day 1.

  But what you're describing is bigger. Once widget exists, it's a product with many ongoing work items:

  ┌───────────────────┬───────────────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Work type     │    Where it comes from    │  Who initiates   │           Pipeline (different shape)           │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Feature           │ Product team / customer   │ Product role     │ intake → plan → build → verify → ship          │
  │                   │ ask                       │                  │                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Bug               │ User report / QA /        │ Support or QA    │ triage → reproduce → fix → verify → ship       │
  │                   │ monitoring                │ role             │                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Incident          │ Production alert          │ Operator /       │ detect → contain → root-cause → fix →          │
  │                   │                           │ oncall           │ postmortem → harden                            │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Support ticket    │ Customer                  │ Support role     │ triage → answer or route to bug/feature        │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Refactor / tech   │ Engineering               │ Engineer role    │ propose → review → build → verify → ship       │
  │ debt              │                           │                  │                                                │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ DevOps / infra    │ Operator                  │ Operator role    │ propose → review → apply → monitor             │
  ├───────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Compliance /      │ Compliance role           │ Compliance       │ gather evidence → report → remediate           │
  │ audit             │                           │                  │                                                │
  └───────────────────┴───────────────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Each has its own intake template, own phase pipeline, and own role choreography. But they all hit the same product — same
   PRD lineage, same Knowledge Graph, same audit trail, same GitHub repo.

  This is what makes Spine "the team": it isn't just an engineer. It's product + eng + QA + DevOps + support, all
  coordinating around one product, each playing their own role with their own pipeline.

  What this implies for the model

  Today's schema has:
  - spine_lifecycle.project (which is really doing double duty — both "the long-lived product" AND "the single work item
  being built right now")

  For what you're describing, we likely need two distinct things:
  
  - Product (long-lived): owns the PRD evolution, the KG, the GitHub repo reference, the running history of
  features+bugs+incidents+etc. There's one of these per real product (one for "widget"; one for "spine itself" if Spine
  ever dogfoods on itself).
  - Work Item (short-lived): the thing currently going through a pipeline. Has a type
  (feature/bug/incident/support/refactor/infra/compliance), an intake template appropriate to that type, a pipeline
  appropriate to that type, a role appropriate to that type, and a back-reference to the Product. Many of these per Product
   over time.
  
  The work-item-type drives:
  - Which intake template runs (plan/templates/intake/bug.yaml vs feature.yaml vs incident.yaml)
  - Which phase pipeline applies (plan/pipelines/bug-pipeline.yaml vs feature-pipeline.yaml)
  - Which roles drive it (Support might triage a bug; QA might verify; Engineer fixes)
  
  This is consistent with spine_flexibility_principle.md ("pipeline-as-data, no SDLC element hardcoded") — pipelines are
  already meant to be customizable. We just haven't built the work-item-type dimension yet.

  Did I get it right?
  
  Specifically:
  - A Product is a long-lived thing (e.g. "widget") that accumulates work items over its lifetime.
  - A Work Item is a unit of work with a type (feature / bug / incident / support / refactor / infra / compliance) that 
  follows its own pipeline.
  - Spine plays whichever role(s) the work item needs — product, engineering, QA, DevOps, support, compliance.
  - All work items for a product share the same KG, GitHub repo, PRD lineage, audit trail.
  
  Yes/no — does that match what you have in mind? And one clarifying question:

  Which work types do you want as first-class on Day 1? I'd start with feature, bug, and incident as the minimum viable set
   (covers ~80% of real SDLC volume); support, refactor, infra, compliance follow when needed. But if you want all 7 from
  the start, that's a bigger initial spec.

  questions about features and fucntionality as well as technical design or infol... so pretty much a great knowledge

⏺ Yes — and this is the piece that turns Spine from a builder into a continuously useful product. Let me play it back:
  
  Q&A / knowledge expert mode

  Beyond "build mode" (intake → plan → build → verify → ship), Spine also has an ask mode. Anyone can query it:
  
  ┌────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Question style       │                  Example                   │           What Spine consults            │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Functional / "what does it │ "How does the client-fee capture logic     │ PRD + KG (find the functions) + actual   │
  │  do"                       │ work?"                                     │ code (via git)                           │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Technical / "where is X"   │ "Where are the email templates?"           │ KG (file/symbol lookup)                  │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Configuration / "how is X  │ "Which database is the analytics service   │ KG + config files (via git read)         │
  │ wired"                     │ connecting to?"                            │                                          │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Design / "why this way"    │ "Why did we pick Postgres over Mongo?"     │ Audit log + TRD + decision history       │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Procedural / "how do we"   │ "How do we deploy?"                        │ Runbooks (planned: docs/ + Operator role │
  │                            │                                            │  memory)                                 │
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Historical / "when did X   │ "When was the auth flow refactored?"       │ Audit log + git commit history at        │
  │ change"                    │                                            │ recorded commits                         │
  └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

  This is fundamentally a retrieval-augmented Q&A across all of Spine's knowledge surfaces. No new pipeline — it's a read
  mode on top of the accumulated knowledge layer.

  How this fits with what's already there

  Spine already has most of the substrate:
  - shared/mcp/tools/kg.py — 9 retrieval tools incl. hybrid_search (vector + graph), find_callers, code_neighborhood,
  doc_for_region, who_owns, find_by_satisfies
  - shared/memory/ — vector-backed lessons (per-role + cross-project)
  - spine_audit.audit_event — every decision recorded
  - The PRD/TRD/Brief lineage I described above
  - lib/role-prompts/memory.md — the Memory role

  What's missing for Q&A:
  - A user-facing entry point: spine ask "<question>" (or a web/Slack/wherever interface)
  - A query orchestration layer that picks the right knowledge surfaces for the question type
  - An LLM synthesis step that turns retrieved snippets into a coherent answer
  - Conversation state (follow-up Q&A is more useful than one-shot)
  
  This isn't a huge new subsystem — it's a Memory role wired up properly with the right MCP tools and a Q&A entry point.

  Full picture so far

  Let me make sure I've got the complete picture before we move to implementation. Spine is:

  1. A long-lived knowledge layer per product — PRD lineage + KG + audit + memory, persistent across sessions and months
  2. A multi-role virtual team — product, engineering, QA, DevOps, support, compliance — each plays its role on each work
  item
  3. A work-item orchestrator — handles features, bugs, incidents, support, refactors, infra, compliance, each with its own
   intake template + phase pipeline + role choreography
  4. Source-of-truth-respecting — GitHub owns the code bytes; Spine remembers (provenance: repo + commit + builder) and
  indexes (KG)
  5. Token-efficient for AI — KG lets agents be surgical: find the 3 functions to modify, not read 50k LOC
  6. A knowledge expert — answer arbitrary functional / technical / design / procedural / historical questions about any
  product Spine has touched

  And underneath all of that, Spine maintains its own authority over the knowledge (PRD/TRD/KG/audit/memory) while
  delegating bytes to GitHub and building to external implementers (humans or AI agents).

  My one question

  Is this the complete picture, or is there one more dimension before we start planning the implementation? I don't want to
   dispatch agents to build piece 1 and then find out piece 8 changes the architecture.

❯ what else are we missing here based on best practices, and the idea and what we are trying to do here which is AI        
  agentic team for buildign any software solution...                                                                       
  
⏺ Good question. Let me think hard. Here's what I'd flag as missing or under-discussed for an enterprise-grade AI agentic
  team — grouped by why each one matters, not by what's easy.

  Things that affect whether Spine is usable by a real org

  1. Multi-user / RBAC / approvals across humans + agents

  Everything today is khash@khash.com. A real team has multiple humans, multiple AI agents, sometimes external auditors.
  Who's allowed to approve a PRD? Who can ship? Who can see customer data? Without RBAC, Spine isn't enterprise-deployable
  in any meaningful sense — and "enterprise-deployable virtual team" is literally line 1 of your positioning memo. This is
  foundational. Includes: identity (humans + agents as first-class), role assignment per work-item, approval-tier gates per
   phase (PRD sign-off ≠ ship gate), and an audit trail of "who approved what when."

  2. Cost / budget management & spend visibility

  AI calls cost money. Without per-product budgets, per-work-item burn-rate alerts, and tier-routing rules ("send the cheap
   question to Haiku; send the security review to Opus"), costs spiral and nobody notices for a month. Spine has
  shared/cost/ (router, classifier, budget_rollup) but it's not surfaced. This is the #1 thing that kills agentic systems 
  in production — uncontrolled token spend.

  3. External integrations (where work comes from + where it goes)

  Spine right now is a closed system you drive via CLI. Real work flows: bugs come from Sentry/GitHub Issues/customers,
  features from Linear/Jira/Slack, incidents from PagerDuty/Datadog. Outputs go back to GitHub PRs, Slack channels,
  customer changelogs. Without bidirectional integrations, Spine is an isolated tool, not a team member. Minimum to be
  useful: GitHub (issues + PRs), Slack (questions + notifications), one ticketing system (Linear or Jira).

  4. Notification routing & on-call

  Bugs need eyes; incidents need pages; PRDs need sign-off. Spine has shared/notify/ (7-channel) but no routing rules,
  on-call schedules, or escalation paths. Without these, notifications are spam or silence — neither works.

  Things that affect whether Spine produces good work

  5. Eval / continuous learning loop

  Does Spine actually get better over time? Are its outputs calibrated? shared/eval/ exists (loader, runner, scorer) and
  shared/calibration/ exists (Platt-scaled) — but they're not running on Spine's own work. Without a continuous eval loop,
  AI quality degrades silently. Spine should be grading its own outputs (PRDs, briefs, build artifacts, verify findings)
  against rubrics, tracking calibration drift, and feeding lessons back into shared/memory/.

  6. Real LLM-driven conductor (5-move intake)

  Already flagged in the drift audit. Today's intake is a deterministic question loop. The 5-move provoke pattern (naive
  cast → provoke → reframe → tier → artifact) exists only in role-prompt text, not the runtime. This is the difference
  between "fancy form" and "actually feels like talking to a senior product person." For an AI team, this matters more than
   any other quality investment.

  7. Retro / lesson capture per work item

  Today nothing auto-runs a retrospective after a work item completes. shared/memory/ is ready to receive lessons but the
  producer side isn't wired. Without this, Spine never accumulates wisdom — every new bug starts from zero context about
  how the team has handled similar bugs before.
  
  Things that affect whether Spine is trustworthy

  8. Reproducibility / replay across Spine versions

  shared/reproducibility/ exists (manifest, replay, diff) but nobody's using it. The drift audit also flagged
  pipeline-version-locking (STORY-1.7.5) as the analogue. Six months from now, when someone asks "why did Spine approve
  this PRD?", we need to be able to replay the exact intake conversation, the exact prompts, the exact model versions, the
  exact retrievals. This is also how you debug AI quality regressions over time.

  9. Safety boundaries / capability flags per role per environment

  What can the AI agents NOT do? Touch production data? Move money? Delete code? Make external calls? Without explicit
  capability flags per role per environment (dev/staging/prod), one prompt injection or one runaway agent is a real
  incident. Real AI deployments fail here.
  
  10. Disaster recovery & continuity

  Spine becomes load-bearing for the team's institutional memory (PRDs, decisions, KG, audit, lessons). If Spine's DB
  corrupts or the box dies, what's the RPO/RTO? Backups? Multi-region? Pipeline-version-aware restore? Without DR, Spine is
   a single point of failure for the team's brain.
  
  What I'd flag as NOT missing (we have it or it's in motion)

  - Pipeline-as-data (sdlc-pipeline.yaml; flexible)
  - Knowledge graph infrastructure (already real; needs more indexing wiring per the drift audit)
  - Audit pipeline (now working since the kg-bug fix in commit 9f99c2a)
  - Org bundle hierarchy (partial — bundles exist, inheritance is on the backlog)
  - Per-role memory (substrate exists; consumer side needs wiring)
  - Verify subsystem (TRON integrated)

  My one question
  
  Of the 10 missing pieces above, which 2-3 do you consider P0 for the "Day 0 — Spine v2.0 is usable by my team" milestone,
   and which are "Day 30 / Day 90 / nice-to-have"?

  I'd push back if your instinct is "all 10 P0" because that's months of work and we'd lose the discipline we've kept so
  far. My own instinct for a minimum-viable-team-deployable Spine: #1 (RBAC), #2 (cost), #3 (GitHub integration only, defer
   Slack/Linear) as P0. #5 (eval), #6 (real intake), #8 (replay) as fast-follow. #4, #7, #9, #10 as second wave. But you
  know your context better than I do.

  Let me play back the complete picture now, including the Hub, and then I'll dispatch a ground-truth audit so I stop
  guessing at "we already have X."

  The architecture, fully restated

  Two-tier role hierarchy:

  SPINE HUB (the AI C-suite — one per organization)
  │
  ├─ CPO role: queries all project Spines' Product Managers
  ├─ CTO role: queries all project Spines' Tech Leads, sees architecture/KGs
  ├─ CCO role: compliance + customer across products
  ├─ COO role: operational health across products
  ├─ Hub Dashboard: live view of every project Spine + what it's doing + on what machine
  ├─ Hub-level KG: cross-product (shared services, common dependencies)
  ├─ Hub Memory: org-wide lessons that span products
  └─ Project registry: each project Spine registers, heartbeats, reports up
      │
      ├─ PROJECT SPINE: widget
      │  ├─ Product Manager role (PMs ARE Spine — there's no human PM)
      │  ├─ Architect / Engineer / QA / DevOps / Support / Compliance roles
      │  ├─ KG of widget's codebase + docs
      │  ├─ Memory (per-role lessons + cross-role)
      │  ├─ Audit log + provenance (GitHub ref + commit)
      │  ├─ Handles work items: feature/bug/incident/support/refactor/infra/compliance
      │  └─ Q&A mode for anyone with access
      │
      ├─ PROJECT SPINE: another-product (same structure)
      └─ ...

  Spine Hub = "the AI-staffed enterprise." A founder/CEO (the human) sets up Hub once. The Hub then orchestrates project
  Spines, each of which is a complete AI team for one product. Hub-level roles (CPO/CTO/CCO/COO) report up across
  portfolio.
  
  Underneath, both tiers respect the same principles:
  - GitHub owns code bytes; Spine owns knowledge (PRD/TRD/KG/audit/memory/provenance)
  - All inter-subsystem communication via MCP (FR-5, which we're already cleaning up)
  - Pipeline-as-data + org bundle hierarchy (Default → Company → Project)
  - Single audit log per project; aggregated views in Hub
  - Source-of-truth for AI agents = the KG (token-efficient surgical lookups)

  The full feature set you want:

  ┌─────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │                              Capability                              │                 Tier                  │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 1   │ RBAC: humans + AI agents as first-class identities, per-phase        │ Hub + Project                         │
  │     │ approval gates                                                       │                                       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 2   │ Cost management: per-product budgets, burn-rate alerts,              │ Hub + Project                         │
  │     │ tier-routing, spend dashboard                                        │                                       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3   │ External integrations: GitHub (issues+PRs+commits), Slack,           │ Project (Hub aggregates)              │
  │     │ Linear/Jira, PagerDuty, Sentry                                       │                                       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 4   │ Notification routing + on-call schedules + escalation paths          │ Hub (rules) + Project (emit)          │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 5   │ Eval / continuous calibration of Spine's own outputs                 │ Hub (cross-product metrics) + Project │
  │     │                                                                      │  (per-run)                            │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 6   │ Real LLM-driven conductor (5-move provoke intake)                    │ Project                               │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 7   │ Retro / lesson capture per work item; memory-grade lessons fed back  │ Project (capture) + Hub               │
  │     │                                                                      │ (cross-product roll-up)               │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 8   │ Reproducibility / replay across Spine versions                       │ Project                               │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 9   │ Safety boundaries / capability flags per role per env                │ Hub (policy) + Project (enforcement)  │
  │     │ (dev/staging/prod)                                                   │                                       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 10  │ Disaster recovery + continuity (backups, multi-region, restore)      │ Hub + Project                         │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 11  │ Q&A knowledge expert mode (per-project + Hub-cross-project)          │ Both                                  │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 12  │ Work-item types: feature / bug / incident / support / refactor /     │ Project                               │
  │     │ infra / compliance                                                   │                                       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 13  │ Re-engagement flow (Day 14: "Spine, add login to widget" → loads     │ Project                               │

⏺ The audit is back. Your instinct was right — I was wrong to say "we have cost management." Owning that.

  The honest score

  Of 27 capabilities: 6 REAL, 11 PARTIAL, 1 STUB, 9 MISSING.

  The shape of the gap matters more than the count.

  What's solidly REAL (6)

  MCP server (27 tools, all dispatch). Audit pipeline (463 rows in production across 4 subsystems — the fix from commit
  9f99c2a is the reason it's writing now). Pipeline-as-data (load→overlay→lock). Bootstrap (make bootstrap, spine doctor).
  Org-bundle inheritance (Default → Company → Project actually applies overlays + stamps sha256). TRON integration (real
  AuditManager calls).
  
  What's PARTIAL — "built but unwired" (11)

  This is the most important pattern. There's a class of code that exists, has real logic, has its DB schema applied, and 
  has ZERO callers:
  
  ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Capability      │                                           Reality                                            │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                     │ Router + V16 ledger schema + 3 rollup views + check-budget CLI all real. 0 rows in           │
  │ Cost management     │ spine_recording.costs. Nothing ever writes a cost row. 5 of the 7 cost modules (classifier,  │
  │                     │ complexity_scorer, team_router, prompt_cache, user_override) have zero imports anywhere.     │
  │                     │ Budget enforcement enforces against an empty table.                                          │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Knowledge Graph     │ Schema applied (5 tables). 9 retrieval tools all real SQL. 0 nodes. 0 edges. 0 embeddings.   │
  │                     │ The indexer was never run. Hybrid search returns empty.                                      │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Calibration         │ Real Platt + banded math + per-role wrappers. 0 rows in spine_calibration.*. Wrappers never  │
  │                     │ called from plan/build/orchestrator.                                                         │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Eval harness        │ Real loader/runner/scorer/aggregator + 2 example datasets. 0 runs ever. No make eval, no CI  │
  │                     │ hook.                                                                                        │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cross-LLM           │ Real Anthropic+OpenAI consensus. Zero from shared.validation imports outside the package     │
  │ validation          │ itself.                                                                                      │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Memory              │ Real pgvector cosine + lesson_indexer + playbook_store. 0 lessons, 0 retrievals. No          │
  │ (vector-backed      │ role-prompt writes or reads.                                                                 │
  │ lessons)            │                                                                                              │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Reproducibility     │ Real RunManifest + replay + diff CLI. Zero callers. ~/.spine/manifests/ is never written.    │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Notifications       │ 7 real channels (Slack/Email/Webhook/etc.). Zero callers outside the module — nothing ever   │
  │                     │ sends.                                                                                       │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Standalone          │                                                                                              │
  │ deployability of    │ Plausible from file inspection, no automated test.                                           │
  │ verify/             │                                                                                              │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Safety capability   │ Real for 3 pipeline-edit caps. Doesn't gate MCP tools, no env-scoping.                       │
  │ flags               │                                                                                              │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Spine Hub dashboard │ shared/ui/dashboard/index.html "SPINE CONTROL CENTER" is real — but it's per-Spine, not      │
  │                     │ cross-Spine. No project registry across machines.                                            │
  └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  What's STUB (1) — already-known

  5-move intake conductor → deterministic question loop. plan/runtime/intake_runner_llm.py does not exist.

  What's MISSING (9) 

  RBAC/identity, GitHub integration, Slack/Linear/Jira/PagerDuty/Sentry integration, notification
  routing/on-call/escalation, DR/backups/multi-region, true Spine Hub (multi-Spine project registry + cross-project
  aggregation), re-engagement (spine project resume), work-item types beyond feature, Q&A entry point.

  One more silent finding worth flagging

  shared/mcp/tools/iso.py builds an AuditRecord and returns its UUID but never calls write_via_psql — every iso_invoke
  audit row is silently dropped. Orchestrator/standards/auditor/kg do persist correctly. So even the working audit pipeline
   has a hole.

  Tests

  Only 2 test files for the entire v2 Python codebase. The "Done" markings in BACKLOG.md are not test-backed.

  --- 
  What this tells us about the work ahead

  The work isn't one shape — it's four: 
  
  1. Wire the unwired (11 PARTIAL): connect cost router to actual emit points (every LLM call, every tool invocation); run
  the KG indexer; have roles write lessons; have orchestrator capture RunManifests; have eval run on each completion.
  Mostly small per-item, big in aggregate. 
  2. Build the missing upper tier (9 MISSING): RBAC, GitHub/Slack/Linear/etc. integrations, on-call routing, capability
  gating per role per env, DR, real Spine Hub, re-engagement, work-item types, Q&A.
  3. Replace the stub (1): LLM-driven 5-move intake conductor.
  4. Test-back the foundation (cross-cutting): build a real test suite so "Done" actually means something.

  That's a real piece of work, but at AI velocity it's hours of dispatched parallel agents, not weeks.

  ---

  per-Spine, not Hub; no project registry exists). The Hub questions still stand:

  - One Hub per org, or can there be multiple (per business unit / region)?
  - Hub ↔ Project Spine communication: pull-on-demand, push-heartbeat, or both?
  - Where does Hub live — same repo with a hub/ subsystem, separate repo, separate deployment target?

  Answer those and I have everything I need to break the 4-shape work into parallel implementation waves and start
  dispatching.

⏺ Agent "R5: Multi-agent orchestration" completed

⏺ R5 (multi-agent orchestration) back. 5 of 10 in. 5 still running.

  R5 is the most architecturally concrete yet. Key takeaways:
  - Spine IS LangGraph-shaped already, just in bash — that's an asset, not a debt to be paid off
  - Recommended tri-layer hybrid: Hub (hierarchical supervisor) + Project (graph state machine — what Spine is today) +
  Within-role (bounded swarm + tool-using). Don't go all-LangGraph; don't go all-swarm.
  - Multi-agent isn't automatically better — Devin's single-supervised-agent choice deserves respect; multi-agent burns
  ~15x tokens (per Anthropic's own published research multi-agent post-mortem)
  - Concrete cross-LLM gating policy: never for orchestration edges; only for content decisions inside a phase; never for
  long-form outputs (AutoGen's documented "agreement spiral" failure mode)
  - Top 5 ships ranked: (1) sampler oversight daemon — 2-3 days, very high ROI; (2) Hub skeleton; (3) LLM-driven conductor;
   (4) compaction checkpoints at phase transitions; (5) cross-LLM debate for architect tech review

  Pattern convergence across the 5 reports so far:
  - All converge on multi-role + audit + cross-project coordination as the moat nobody else has — R3 calls it auditability,
   R2 calls it governance-by-architecture, R5 calls it cross-project orchestration, R8 calls it "AI org chart"
  - All flag the substrate-exists-but-not-wired pattern from the ground-truth audit 
  - All recommend keeping the bash core, never replacing
