❯ explain to me what is this for                                                                                           

⏺ Fair question — my last message went technical-jargon-heavy without explaining the purpose. Let me back up and explain
  plainly what we're deciding.

  What identity / authentication is FOR in Spine

  It's the answer to "who's asking?" When a human interacts with the Hub UI, Spine needs to know:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Job                    │                           Why Spine needs it                            │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Login — prove who you are                │ So Spine doesn't let just anyone with the URL approve a production      │
  │                                          │ deploy                                                                  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ User account — a record of "Khash" or    │ So the audit log says "approved by alice@acme.com at 14:32", not        │
  │ "alice@acme.com"                         │ "approved by ???"                                                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Session — remember you're logged in for  │ So you don't have to re-login every 5 minutes                           │
  │ a while                                  │                                                                         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Permissions — what you're allowed to do  │ So the intern can view but only the CTO can approve a $50k deploy       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Multi-user invitations — your cofounder, │ So a team can share one Hub without sharing one login                   │
  │  your engineer, your CSM all use Spine   │                                                                         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                          │ So a Fortune 500 employee can log into Spine using their existing       │
  │ Single sign-on (SSO) for enterprises     │ Okta/Azure AD credentials — corporate IT enforces password policy + MFA │
  │                                          │  + offboarding centrally                                                │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  Why this matters specifically for Spine

  Several things we already locked in REQUIRE identity to work:

  - AI scrum master pings the business user (Decision #5) — Spine needs to know WHO the business user IS to ping them
  - Communication preferences per-user (Decision #6) — Spine needs to know which user wants Slack vs SMS vs email
  - Two-tier role authority + override (Decision #8) — Spine needs to know WHO can override what
  - Vault access with two-party audit (Decision #9) — Spine logs WHO requested each secret, requires verified identity
  - Federation consent (Decision #10) — when a parent Hub registers a child Hub, the consent UX needs to know WHICH admin
  granted consent
  - Feature-flag licensing (Decision #23) — license bundle says "this feature enabled for these users/groups", requires
  identity
  - SOC 2 / compliance evidence (Decision #24) — auditors require per-user attribution on every change

  Without identity, none of those features can function properly. So identity is the substrate — like Postgres or audit log
   — that everything else sits on.

  What the decision is actually about

  It's NOT "should we have identity" (we obviously must). It's about how thick a system we build for it on Day 1:

  - Thick (Keycloak/Ory embedded): ship a full enterprise IdP server alongside Hub. Customer gets everything (multi-IdP
  federation, social login, fancy permission models) out of the box. Heavy, more containers to run, more for customer admin
   to learn.
  - Minimum + scaffold (what I recommended as D): ship just enough for v1.0 to work (users, groups, password login, simple
  permissions). Build SAML/OIDC/SCIM later when customer demand surfaces. Lighter, faster to ship, expandable.

  It's the same pattern we've used everywhere else: build the source of truth + scaffold the interface; defer fancy
  adapters until customer demand surfaces.

  Re-asking simply

  For v1.0, do you want:
  - Heavy from Day 1 — embed a full identity system like Keycloak so everything's available out of the box (more upfront
  work, more capability)
  - Minimum + scaffold — basic login + users + permissions now; add fancy SSO/SAML/Active Directory federation when a real
  customer asks for it (less upfront work, faster to ship, expandable)
  
  My rec is the minimum + scaffold (consistent with how we handled everything else). But happy to commit harder to the
  embedded-Keycloak route if you want enterprise IdP support shipped Day 1.

✻ Cogitated for 16s
  
❯ thicker the better with option to lighten it up as needed                                                                

⏺ Locked. Keycloak embedded + feature-flag lightening is the right call — identity is one of the few systems where retrofit
   cost is genuinely high, so erring toward more capability upfront is correct.

  FINALIZED DESIGN DECISIONS (updated)

  1-24. (unchanged)
  25. Identity / authentication: Keycloak embedded by default, lightening via feature flags. Keycloak ships as a sibling
  container alongside Hub (Java/JVM dep accepted as the cost of mature feature set + enterprise-recognized name + 10+ years
   of production hardening). Spine Hub uses Keycloak as its OIDC provider — Hub never directly handles
  SAML/SCIM/social-login/MFA logic; it delegates everything to Keycloak. Customer's existing IdP (Okta, Azure AD, Google
  Workspace, Ping, OneLogin) federates into Keycloak as a brokered upstream IdP. Spine Hub trusts only Keycloak.

  Feature flags control which Keycloak capabilities are exposed per tier:
  - Free / laptop: single realm, basic username+password, no MFA enforcement, no IdP federation
  - Founder (BYOC): MFA optional, social login enabled, single IdP federation allowed
  - Team: MFA required, multi-IdP federation, basic SCIM
  - Enterprise: full SCIM 2.0, multi-realm, advanced password policy, custom themes, audit export
  - Air-gapped: works fully (Keycloak has no external dependencies); social login disabled by default
  
  Day-0 wizard installs Keycloak + generates initial admin credentials + creates default realm + configures Hub as a
  Keycloak OIDC client. Admin UI is Keycloak's own (mature; well-documented). Per lighten as needed: customer can later
  swap to embedded Ory Kratos / external Okta-as-IdP / whatever, since Hub only talks OIDC to "whichever provider" —
  Keycloak is the well-tested default, not a lock-in.

  - ✅ Identity / auth (#25 — Keycloak embedded, feature-flag lightening)

  - Demo / launch sequence — depends on product readiness; revisit when v1.0 is approaching ship
  - Pricing tiers + specific $ — defer until product is built and tested
  
  Naturally answered by the all-AI commitment:

  I think we're at the natural endpoint of pure design decisions. The next phase is execution — walking the codebase per
  #22, marking each artifact KEEP / REFACTOR / REBUILD / BUILD-NEW / DELETE against the locked design, then actually doing

  DECISION 12 of N — Anything I've missed?

  Before we transition from "designing Spine" to "building Spine against the locked design," one straightforward question:
  Are there any design decisions I haven't asked about that you want to lock in first?

  - Spine company business structure (Delaware C-corp? LLC? Solo prop?) — affects fundraising, hiring (if you ever change

  - IP / patent strategy — do we file anything? Closed-source means our innovations stay protected by trade secret; patents

  - Legal: closed-source means we need an EULA, customer contracts (MSA template), DPA template, AUP. Do these exist
  anywhere yet?
  - The "Spine company" itself's vault, license signing keys, SOC2 evidence pipeline — these are operational from Day 1;
  not really "decisions" but worth knowing where they live
  
  None of these are blocking the codebase triage. But if any of them affect what we build, better to surface now.

  Anything else on your mind for design — or are we ready to transition to the codebase triage and the actual build?

  ---
  What's next — transition to execution
  
  Per Decision #22, the bridge from design to build is the per-artifact codebase triage. The job is to walk the existing
  16-commit codebase (which was built before many of these design decisions were locked) and classify each artifact against
   the new locked architecture as KEEP / REFACTOR / REBUILD / BUILD-NEW / DELETE.

  Here's how I'd approach it:

  Approach: Parallel triage agents per subsystem, then aggregate.

  ┌──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐
  │      Agent       │                           Scope                           │               Output               │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                  │ shared/{mcp, audit, standards, calibration, cost, eval,   │ Triage marks per file +            │
  │ T1: shared/      │ memory, notify, reproducibility, validation, schemas,     │ justification per the 26 decisions │
  │                  │ skills, api, ui}                                          │                                    │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T2: plan/        │ plan/{artifacts, pipeline, runtime, swarm, templates,     │ Same                               │
  │                  │ decomposer}                                               │                                    │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T3: build/       │ build/{kg, runtime, bridge, migration}                    │ Same                               │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                  │ verify/tron — mostly evaluate boundary with new           │                                    │
  │ T4: verify/      │ architecture, not internal TRON code (which is            │ Same                               │
  │                  │ subtree-merged from a separate product)                   │                                    │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T5:              │                                                           │ Same — much of lib/ is v1-era;     │
  │ orchestrator/ +  │ orchestrator/{lib, bin, state} + lib/* (v1 daemon system) │ needs honest evaluation against v2 │
  │ lib/             │                                                           │  commitments                       │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T6: infra + docs │ db/, tools/, docs/, recipes/, Makefile, requirements.txt, │ Same                               │
  │  + tools         │  .gitignore, etc.                                         │                                    │
  └──────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘

  Each agent gets the full 26-decision tracker as context, walks their subsystem, and produces a structured triage doc with
   these columns: path | current purpose | marking | justification | depends on (other artifacts) | target completion order
   (1=foundational, 3=builds on others).
  
  I aggregate the 6 reports into a single docs/v3-TRIAGE.md document that becomes the canonical execution plan. Then we
  work the BUILD-NEW and REBUILD lists in dependency order.

  This takes ~30-60 minutes wall-clock (6 agents in parallel). The output is the execution roadmap — what gets rebuilt, in
  what order, against the locked design. 

  Question: Should I dispatch the 6-agent codebase triage now, or do you want to adjust the approach first (different agent
   split, different output format, sequential instead of parallel, anything else)?

✻ Worked for 18s

❯ The one thing we never talked about is "SMART SPINE"  self learning, self improving, smart SPINE.. how does smart get    
  better over time for each enterprise that uses it and for spine as a product                                             
  
⏺ You're right — this is a major missing dimension we haven't addressed. Self-learning / self-improving Spine is arguably
  the most strategic differentiator of all, because it's the one thing every other AI dev tool LACKS today. Cursor doesn't
  get smarter at your codebase. Devin starts fresh every session. Copilot's "context" is what fits in the prompt window.
  None of them have a real learning loop.

  Spine HAS substrate for this (most of it sitting unwired per the ground-truth audit) — but we've never designed the
  actual learning architecture. Let me lay it out, then ask the foundational decision.

  The three tiers of "Smart Spine"

  Tier 1: Per-deployment learning (each customer's Spine gets smarter at THEIR stuff)

  What it learns at customer X:
  - Codebase — KG of code, calling patterns, naming conventions, anti-patterns specific to this codebase
  - Decisions — which proposals got approved/rejected, why, by whom, with what reasoning
  - Team preferences — verbose vs terse PRDs, formal vs casual, risk-averse vs aggressive
  - Domain context — financial-services jargon vs healthcare jargon; their specific business rules
  - Tooling preferences — Linear not Jira; Datadog not New Relic; AWS not Azure
  - Past incidents — runbook accumulation: "last time we saw this error, the fix was X"
  - Common bugs — patterns of issues in this customer's code (don't repeat)
  - Process drift — "this team always rejects deploys on Fridays; auto-defer"
  
  All lives in customer's own deployment (their KG, their memory, their audit chain). Zero cross-customer leakage. Spine
  instance at month 12 is dramatically more valuable than at month 1 because it KNOWS this customer's context.

  Substrate exists: shared/memory/ (vector-backed lessons), shared/calibration/ (per-role Platt), KG (spine_kg), audit
  chain (working).
  What's missing: writer hooks (capture lessons from audit events), retrieval at role-action time (every role queries
  memory before acting), the calibration loop (outcomes flow back), the indexer actually running on customer code.

  Tier 2: Cross-deployment learning (vendor learns anonymized patterns across customers)

  What the vendor learns from aggregated signal:
  - "Architect role's TRD proposals get approved 78% when they cite KG impact-radius; 45% when they don't" → update role
  charter to require KG citation
  - "The 5-move intake succeeds when business user is decisive; fails when uncertain → add a 'tier the uncertainty' step"
  - "Customers running React frontends see X% more code-quality issues on Y pattern → tune ISO scanner"
  - "Cross-LLM consensus disagrees most often on Z type of decision → adjust which decisions require consensus"
  - "When auditor rejects a build, the most common root cause is K → update engineer role to check for K"
  
  This is how Spine, as a product, gets better. Every release ships improved role charters / pipeline defaults / bundle
  presets based on cross-customer patterns. Customers benefit from each other without sharing data. Updates flow through
  the federation tree (Decision #16) — vendor publishes improved bundles; customers approve rollouts.

  Privacy model: opt-in per customer, opt-out granular per data class (some customers may share calibration outcomes but
  not memory contents), full anonymization (no customer-identifying data ever leaves; only statistical patterns),
  federated-learning-style aggregation where feasible. Customer's audit log captures what was shared and when.
  
  Tier 3: Spine's self-improvement (Spine improves itself, eating its own dogfood)

  - Spine's own development is a Spine project — runs in Spine's own Hub; produces lessons; calibrates; evolves
  - Each release teaches the next — lessons from v1.0 inform v1.1 design
  - Drift audits we've been running this conversation are literally Tier 3 already happening — Spine evaluating Spine
  - The eval harness runs on every release — Spine grades itself against the golden corpus
  - Master roles author improvements to lower-tier role charters based on observed patterns

  This is the meta-loop that prevents Spine from getting stale. AI dev tools without this rot — the prompts written in 2024
   become embarrassing in 2026.

  Why this is a category-defining differentiator

  Every AI dev tool today is stateless across customers and stateless across sessions within a customer. Cursor doesn't
  know what you decided yesterday. Devin doesn't know what your last 5 customers preferred. They start over each time.

  Spine with Smart-Spine architecture is the first product where:
  - Customer's Spine appreciates over time — month 12 >> month 1 in value at the same customer
  - Switching cost is real (positive lock-in) — leaving Spine means losing accumulated learning; not malicious lock-in
  (data is portable) but genuine "starting over loses value"
  - The product gets smarter from every customer — vendor learns from aggregate patterns and ships improvements
  - The pitch becomes: "Spine is the AI engineering team that's been with you for 3 years. It knows your codebase, your 
  conventions, your customers. The longer you use it, the harder it is to switch."
  
  That's the SaaS-vendor-moat narrative applied to an AI dev tool — but uniquely possible only because Spine has the audit
  chain + KG + memory + calibration substrate that nobody else does.

  What needs to be designed for this
  
  Roughly the work to make Spine smart:
  1. Memory writer hooks (~7 trigger points per R4's research)
  2. Memory retrieval at role-action time (every role queries memory + KG before acting)
  3. Calibration outcome capture loop (verify pass/fail; auditor approve/reject; user approve/reject — all feed back)
  4. KG indexer actually running (currently 0 nodes — has to fire on every commit)
  5. Lesson promotion ladder (per-role → per-project → per-org → cross-org with consent)
  6. Anonymized telemetry consent + plumbing for Tier 2
  7. Update bundle authoring at vendor side (Master roles in vendor's Spine produce improvements)
  8. Eval harness running on every release (Spine grading itself)
  9. Retro role wired up (currently stub in phases.yaml; should auto-fire per work-item completion → produce lessons)
  10. Cross-LLM consensus as a learning signal (disagreement = data, not just an error) 
  
  Substrate for most of this already exists. The DESIGN work is wiring + the privacy model for Tier 2.

  DECISION 12 of N — How aggressive should cross-customer learning be?

  This is the foundational call because it has privacy + trust + competitive implications. Tier 1 (per-deployment) is
  obviously yes — every customer's Spine learns about their own stuff; nobody disputes that. Tier 3 (Spine
  self-improvement) is obviously yes — that's just us improving our own product.
  
  The real fork is Tier 2 — vendor learning from anonymized cross-customer patterns:

  Option: A. NO cross-customer learning, ever
  Stance: Vendor never aggregates anything from customers. Pure privacy. Each Spine is fully isolated. Vendor improvements
    come from vendor's own dogfood + customer-submitted feedback + market research.
  Implication: Matches the absolute privacy stance ("Spine never holds your data, never sees your patterns"). Loses the
    cross-customer-improvement loop. Updates rely on vendor's own observations, not real-world signal. Slower product
    evolution.
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Option: B. Opt-in anonymized patterns
  Stance: Customer can opt into anonymized telemetry — calibration outcomes, role-success rates, pattern frequencies.
    Granular opt-in per data class. No raw data, no customer-identifying info — only statistical aggregates. Vendor
    publishes improvements; customers see what was used.
  Implication: Best of both: customers who care about privacy can opt out fully; customers who want a smarter product over
    time opt in. Vendor builds the cross-customer learning engine. Standard pattern (HashiCorp telemetry, GitHub usage
    analytics, Datadog signals — all opt-in).
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Option: C. Opt-out anonymized patterns
  Stance: Telemetry is ON by default; customer can opt out. Vendor gets faster signal; customers who don't care benefit by
    default.
  Implication: Faster signal aggregation. Some enterprises will refuse — "everything is opt-in or we don't deploy." Risks
    the "Spine never sees your stuff" pitch. Federated learning still keeps raw data local.
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Option: D. Federated learning only (no central aggregation)
  Stance: True federated learning — model updates flow between customer Hubs and vendor without raw data ever centralizing.

    Each customer's Hub trains/refines locally; only weight deltas flow up.
  Implication: Strongest privacy guarantee. Hardest engineering. Probably overkill for v1.0; relevant for the
  regulated-bank
    tier later.

  My recommendation: B (opt-in anonymized). Matches the "Spine never holds your data" pitch while preserving the
  cross-customer-improvement loop. Customers who care about absolute privacy opt out (no penalty). Customers who want Spine
   to keep getting smarter benefit from collective signal. Vendor's role-charter improvements + bundle defaults get
  published through the federation update mechanism (#16) with full transparency about what informed them.

  A, B, C, or D for Tier 2 cross-customer learning?

✻ Brewed for 37s

❯ any cross-customer should be permissioned only.. cross orgniazation can be turn on or off, within a hub is also turn     
  on/off but default on...                                                                                                 
  
⏺ Locked — and the within-Hub-default-ON, cross-org-default-OFF distinction is the right model. Matches how real
  enterprises actually work: internal knowledge sharing is encouraged; external sharing is gated.

  FINALIZED DESIGN DECISIONS (updated)

  1-26. (unchanged)
  27. Smart Spine — three-tier learning architecture with explicit consent boundaries:

  Tier: 1a. Per-project Spine (intra-project)
  What learns: Each project Spine learns from its own work — codebase patterns, prior decisions, role outcomes, calibration
  Default: Always on (obviously)
  Override: N/A
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: 1b. Within-Hub (cross-project, within one customer's org)
  What learns: Lessons + patterns flow between project Spines under the same Hub federation tree. Master roles aggregate.
    Bundles get refined. Marketing's Scrum Master learns from Finance's Scrum Master.
  Default: DEFAULT ON. Customer admin can disable via bundle override (for joint ventures / legally-isolated subsidiaries /

    strict project isolation needs).
  Override: Bundle setting learning.within_hub: enabled (default true)
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: 2. Cross-organization (vendor aggregates anonymized patterns across customers)
  What learns: Vendor learns statistical patterns across all customers; publishes improved role charters / bundles /
  scanner
    rules via the federation update mechanism (#16)
  Default: DEFAULT OFF. Customer must explicitly opt in via Hub UI consent flow. Granular opt-in per data class
    (calibration-outcomes vs role-success-rates vs pattern-frequencies). Anonymized + aggregated only; no raw data.
  Override: Bundle setting learning.cross_org.consent: false (default)
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: 3. Vendor self-improvement
  What learns: Vendor's own Spine deployment improves the product. Eats its own dogfood. Drift audits, calibration,
    eval-on-every-release.
  Default: Always on (vendor's own choice for their own deployment)
  Override: N/A

  This is the most defensible privacy posture possible while still preserving real cross-customer learning for opt-in 
  customers. Matches the "Spine never holds your data" stance — for cross-org, customer's data never leaves unless they
  explicitly consent; only their statistical signal does, anonymized.
  
  What this requires in the build (all becomes explicit must-build for v1.0):

  - Memory writer hooks — wire R4's 7 trigger points (audit_event → lesson) — within-Hub learning depends on this
  - Memory retrieval at every role action — query memory + KG before acting; cite sources per the Cite-or-Refuse contract
  - Calibration outcome capture — every verify pass/fail, every auditor approve/reject, every user approve/reject feeds
  spine_calibration.outcome table → weekly Platt refit
  - KG indexer firing — on every commit, every directive, every artifact (currently 0 nodes — has to actually run)
  - Master role aggregation — Master Scrum Master / Master Architect / etc. periodically aggregate child Hub lessons;
  publish refined playbooks; distribute via bundle inheritance
  - Lesson promotion ladder — per-role → per-project → per-Hub → (with consent) → vendor cross-org
  - Anonymized telemetry pipeline — for Tier 2: pattern extraction + sanitization + aggregation + transmission + audit
  trail of what was shared
  - Update bundle authoring at vendor — Master roles in vendor's own Spine produce improvements; signed bundles flow
  through federation tree
  - Eval harness on every release — golden suite runs; results in audit; regressions block release
  - Retro role wired up — currently stub in phases.yaml; auto-fires per work-item completion; produces structured lessons
  - Cross-LLM consensus as learning signal — disagreement is data; feeds calibration corpus
  - "Spine knows you" UX — Master roles can surface to the human: "Your team has rejected the last 3 proposals like this; 
  want me to adjust my approach?" The PROACTIVE smart-team behavior

  This is the "AI org that's been with you for 3 years" pitch made architecturally real. Cursor / Devin / Factory / Copilot
   — none of them have this loop. Smart Spine is the wedge that gets harder for competitors to copy over time because it
  compounds: month 12 Spine at your customer is dramatically more valuable than month 1, AND month 12 Spine (the product)
  is dramatically more capable than month 1 product.

  ---
  Before triage — anything else missing?
  
  Smart Spine was a major dimension I hadn't addressed and you correctly surfaced. Before I dispatch the codebase triage
  (which assumes the design is locked), one last sweep — is there anything else you want to add or lock that we haven't 
  covered?

  Things I'd ask about explicitly if I were you, in case any of them strike you:
  - Internationalization / multi-language support — Spine UI and role-prompts in English-only Day 1, or multi-language?
  - Mobile / native apps — web Hub only Day 1, or should there be iOS/Android apps for approvals on the go (decision queue
  + briefings)?
  - Voice / phone integration — Master Scrum Master can be called via phone for critical escalations?
  - Marketplace for community-contributed role charters / bundles / integrations — long-term play; defer or commit?
  - API surface for third-party integrations — beyond the MCP server; should there be a public Hub REST API for
  customer-built automation?
  - A "Spine for Spine" recursive use case — when the vendor itself uses Spine to build Spine (which is what we're doing
  now), is there anything special about that?
  - Disaster recovery for the Hub itself — what happens when the customer's Hub goes down (since it's the management
  surface for their entire AI team)?
  - Migration tools — for customers coming from competing tools (Devin, Cursor, Factory) — anything to import / map?
  
  If any of those strike you OR there's anything else on your mind, lock it now. If not, I dispatch the 6-agent codebase
  triage.

  FINALIZED DESIGN DECISIONS (updated)
  
  1-27. (unchanged)
  28. Mobile / native apps: SCAFFOLD for v1.0. Define the mobile-API surface (REST endpoints for approvals + briefings +
  status); build placeholder iOS/Android project structure with signing certs and Apple/Google developer accounts; ship a
  web-mobile-responsive Hub UI that works on phones via browser. Native mobile apps deferred to v1.1+ — the responsive web
  covers most "approve on the go" use cases initially.
  29. Voice / phone integration: SCAFFOLD for v1.0. Define the voice-integration interface (which decisions can be
  voice-approved, which roles can be voice-reachable). Wire one provider stub (Twilio probably — most established voice
  API). Defer actual voice flows to v1.1+ until customer demand surfaces (likely "Master CTO callable for critical
  incidents" first).
  30. API + MCP surface: SCAFFOLD for v1.0 — but heavier than the others. Public Hub REST API with OpenAPI 3.x spec + auth
  (via Keycloak per #25) + rate limiting + versioning (v1 from Day 1; reserve v2/v3 namespaces). MCP server surface for
  AI-tool integration. The API and MCP surfaces are foundational — they're what makes Spine programmable +
  integration-friendly + ecosystem-extensible. Per the same pattern: scaffold the framework, ship the contract, build
  adapters/endpoints as needed.
  31. Disaster recovery: BUILD properly for v1.0 (not scaffold). Auto-recovery + immediate notification + tested restore.
  Detailed below.
  
  Spine-for-Spine recursive use case — what made me ask
  
  I brought it up because the recursion has some genuinely interesting properties worth being explicit about:
  
  - Vendor's Spine instance is at the root of the federation update tree (#16) — its Master roles produce the
  bundles/role-charters/improvements that flow down to all customer Hubs
  - The bootstrapping problem: Spine v1.0 doesn't exist yet; vendor is currently using Claude Code orchestration to build
  it. At some point, vendor's Spine starts orchestrating Spine v2 development; eventually Spine is fully self-building
  (with you as the only human directing strategy)
  - Trust circularity: how do we prove Spine works? Spine's audit chain shows Spine made decisions that produced correct
  outputs (verified by Spine's verify subsystem). It's a self-validating loop. This is actually a POWERFUL marketing
  artifact — "watch our public audit log; every commit was reviewed by Spine, signed by an HMAC token, calibrated against 
  Spine's golden corpus, and passed Spine's 7-layer verify — including this one"
  - Eat-your-own-dogfood economics: every improvement to Spine reduces vendor's engineering cost AND becomes a
  customer-facing capability. Aligned incentives — vendor literally cannot ship features that make Spine worse.
  
  Is there anything to lock? I don't think it needs a separate design decision — the recursive case is mostly captured by
  #16 (vendor as update tree root), #21 (all AI all the time), and #27 (vendor self-improvement is Tier 3 of Smart Spine).
  The interesting thing is that all the principles we've locked apply equally to the vendor's own deployment — which makes
  vendor's Spine the proving ground for every feature before it ships to customers.

  If you want to lock anything specific (like "vendor's Spine is publicly observable as a trust-building artifact" or
  "every Spine release must have been built BY a Spine that has been built by the prior Spine"), name it. Otherwise this is
   just an implicit consequence of the other decisions.

  DR design — what we actually build
  
  32. Disaster recovery architecture for v1.0:

  Layer: Container-level auto-recovery
  What we build: K8s deployment with replicas + liveness/readiness probes + auto-restart. Watchdog daemon for non-K8s 
    (laptop, single-host) deployments — already exists as lib/watchdog.sh substrate.
  RPO / RTO target: 30s recovery from container death
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Process-level supervision
  What we build: Each role daemon supervised; auto-restart on crash; circuit breaker on flapping. Existing lib/watchdog.sh
    extended.
  RPO / RTO target: 30s recovery from role-daemon crash
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Data backup — continuous
  What we build: Postgres WAL + Vault snapshots + KG state continuously backed up to customer-chosen S3-compatible storage
    (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, MinIO, Wasabi). Encrypted at rest with KMS. Per bundle policy: retention window (default 30d,
    configurable).    
  RPO / RTO target: RPO ≤ 5 min
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Data restore — tested
  What we build: Documented + automated restore procedure. Periodic restore-to-throwaway-environment verification job 
    (weekly default) — catches "backups exist but restore is broken" failure mode that hits every team. Audit-logs every
    restore test.
  RPO / RTO target: RTO ≤ 30 min for full Hub restoration
  ──────────────────────────────────────── 
  Layer: Heartbeat protocol
  What we build: Each Hub heartbeats to itself (liveness) + to federation parent (if any) + to vendor's status registry
    (opt-in; for proactive support). Failure → multi-medium notification per communication preferences (#6) —
    Slack/SMS/email/PagerDuty/etc.
  RPO / RTO target: Detection ≤ 1 min 
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Federation autonomy
  What we build: Per #10 — if parent Hub is down, child Hubs keep working autonomously. If child Hub is down, parent flags
    it but other children unaffected. No cascading failures.
  RPO / RTO target: Always-on at child Hub level
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Cross-region replication
  What we build: Optional per bundle policy (enterprise tier feature flag). Active-passive: standby replica in second 
    region; promotes on primary failure. Active-active: deferred to v1.1+ (CAP-theorem complications).
  RPO / RTO target: RPO ≤ 5 min, RTO ≤ 10 min for active-passive
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Vault unseal recovery
  What we build: Shamir secret-sharing (3-of-5 humans for high-security) OR cloud-KMS auto-unseal (AWS KMS / Azure KV / GCP

    KMS) — customer chooses at setup wizard. Recovery runbook auto-generated.
  RPO / RTO target: Manual human intervention if Shamir; auto if KMS
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Customer-accidentally-deleted-Hub recovery
  What we build: Soft-delete with 7d retention; restore via Hub UI or vendor support; full deletion requires HMAC-signed
    double-confirmation.
  RPO / RTO target: Recoverable for 7d
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Vendor update infrastructure DR
  What we build: Vendor's own update publishing has DR (CDN-fronted artifact registry, multi-region, signed bundles). If
    vendor's infra is down, customers keep running current version (no auto-degradation).
  RPO / RTO target: Customers unaffected by vendor outage
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: DR runbook
  What we build: Auto-generated per deployment based on actual configuration. Updated when bundle or topology changes.
    Includes: pager rotation, recovery commands, escalation paths, RPO/RTO, last-tested date.
  RPO / RTO target: Always current
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Layer: Backup verification on every release
  What we build: When vendor publishes a new Spine version, customer's automated DR test re-validates restore against the
    new version. Catches "upgrade broke backup compat" before it matters.
  RPO / RTO target: Continuous

  What's deferred to v1.1+:
  - Multi-cloud failover (active in AWS, standby in GCP) — hard problem; rare requirement; defer
  - Real-time hot-standby with sub-second failover — most customers accept warm standby
  
  What this means architecturally: DR isn't a feature; it's a property of every component. Hub container, Vault, Postgres,
  KG, project Spines — each has its DR story built in. The heartbeat protocol + notification routing wires them together.
  The "always notify immediately" requirement is satisfied by routing through the communication-preferences engine
  (Decision #6) — customer's chosen mediums get the alert within 60 seconds.

✻ Cooked for 51s

❯ It also is very important to have migration tools                                                                        

⏺ Critical addition — and "migration" actually covers 4 distinct concerns that each need their own treatment. Let me lay
  them out, then lock.

  What "migration tools" actually means for Spine

  Concern: A. Onboarding migration
  What it is: Importing customer's existing data into Spine — code from GitHub/GitLab, issues from Linear/Jira, docs from
    Confluence/Notion/Google Docs, existing standards/runbooks/wikis
  Why it matters: Without this, new Spine deployments are blind to history. Customer's accumulated context vanishes on Day
  1 
    — kills adoption.
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Concern: B. Spine portability
  What it is: Moving a Spine deployment between shapes (laptop → BYOC → self-hosted → on-prem) or between clouds (AWS →
  GCP)
    without losing audit chain, KG, role charters, bundle config, vault secret refs, memory, lessons, project history
  Why it matters: This is the "no lock-in" property you've emphasized throughout. Customers must be able to LEAVE without
    losing what they built. Counterintuitively, this drives adoption — "we're never trapped" is what gets the CISO yes.
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Concern: C. Software migration as a work-item type
  What it is: Spine helps the customer migrate THEIR product — Python 3.8 → 3.12, React 17 → 18, MySQL → Postgres, monolith

    → microservices, on-prem → cloud
  Why it matters: Falls under the work-item-type design (#19) we already locked. Just needs a "migration" intake template +

    pipeline variant. Specialized but not a new architecture.
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Concern: D. Spine version migrations
  What it is: When Spine vendor publishes v2.0 with breaking changes, customer's v1.0 deployment needs to migrate cleanly —

    DB schemas, bundle formats, role-charter versions, vault namespace structures
  Why it matters: Mandatory engineering hygiene for any long-lived product. Without it, upgrades break customer
  deployments.

  What we build vs scaffold vs defer

  For each:
  
  A (Onboarding migration) — SCAFFOLD + 2 connectors Day 1. First-time-setup wizard includes "import from existing tools"
  step. Connector interface defined for: GitHub, GitLab, Linear, Jira, Confluence, Notion, Google Docs, Asana, Monday,
  Trello, ClickUp, Zendesk, Intercom, Datadog (incident history), Sentry (incident history). Build 2 Day 1: GitHub (90%+ of
   customers have it) + Linear OR Jira (pick whichever the first design partner uses; the other follows in v1.1). Others
  built on customer demand. Import populates: KG (docs + code), initial work items, initial project structure.

  B (Spine portability) — BUILD properly Day 1. This is non-negotiable for the "no lock-in" property to be real.
  Specifically:
  - Export: structured signed tarball containing — audit chain (with hash chain intact for verification), KG dump (nodes +
  edges + properties), bundle config (signed), role charters, vault secret REFERENCES (paths only; never values — the vault
   stays with customer), memory contents (lessons + retrievals), project metadata, federation state, capability flags,
  license status
  - Import: reverse process. Validates signatures + content hashes. Refuses to import if integrity check fails. Restores
  state into a target Hub deployment.
  - Use cases: deployment-shape migrations (laptop → BYOC managed → customer-cloud), cloud migrations (AWS → GCP), DR
  restore, "we're trying Spine on a side project; promote it to our main deployment" workflows, Spine version upgrades that
   require full state recreation
  - Audit trail: every export/import is logged in audit chain with what was moved + signature + actor
  
  C (Migration as work-item type) — DEFERRED via existing work-item type infrastructure. No new architecture needed. v1.1+
  adds a "migration" intake template (specific questions: source system, target system, data volume, cutover constraints,
  rollback requirements, parallel-run period) + pipeline variant (assess → plan → parallel-build → cutover → verify →
  retire-old). Reuses the 7-work-item-type substrate (#19) — migrations are essentially structured feature-work with extra
  phases.

  D (Spine version migrations) — BUILD properly Day 1. Mandatory for any closed-source enterprise product. Every
  schema/format change needs a migration path:
  - DB schemas: Flyway handles this (already in use; just discipline)
  - Bundle format versions: bundle schema has version: field; migration scripts handle format upgrades; downgrades blocked
  - Role-charter format: same — versioned + auto-migrate on upgrade
  - Vault namespace structure: rare but happens — migration scripts run during upgrade
  - KG schema versions: indexer respects version; backfills if format changed
  - Update flow integration (#16): when vendor publishes a release that requires migrations, the changelog declares them;
  customer admin sees "this update will run the following migrations: …" before approving; migrations run during apply;
  rollback path available if migration fails
  - Cross-version compatibility window: vendor commits to supporting upgrades from N-2 versions (so customer on v1.0 can
  upgrade directly to v1.2 even if they skipped v1.1)

  FINALIZED DESIGN DECISIONS (updated)

  1-32. (unchanged)
  33. Migration tooling — 4 concerns, distinct treatments:
  - A. Onboarding migration: scaffold the connector interface + ship GitHub + (Linear OR Jira) Day 1. Wizard step in
  first-time-setup. Others (Confluence/Notion/Asana/etc.) built on customer demand.
  - B. Spine portability (export/import full Spine state): build properly Day 1. Non-negotiable for the "no lock-in" claim
  to be real. Signed tarball format; integrity-verified; auditable.
  - C. Software-migration-as-work-type (Spine helps customer migrate their product): captured by existing work-item type
  design (#19); add specialized intake template + pipeline variant in v1.1.
  - D. Spine version migrations (Spine itself upgrading cleanly): build properly Day 1. Required engineering hygiene.
  Includes DB / bundle / role-charter / vault-namespace / KG-schema migration paths. Wired into update-distribution flow
  (#16) with customer-admin approval gate per migration. N-2 cross-version compatibility commitment.

  Why this matters strategically
  
  The portability piece (B) is the structural answer to enterprise procurement's "what's our exit strategy?" question.
  Closed-source + self-hosted often raises "what if your company goes away?" anxiety. The answer: "export your full state 
  at any time; the audit chain proves it's complete; recreate on whatever you want. We've explicitly designed Spine so you 
  can leave."

  This is the same trust play as OSS-with-portable-data (Linear/Notion can do this) but applied to a closed-source product.
   The export format becomes a marketing artifact — published spec, available as a sample download, demonstrably
  round-trippable. "Try Spine; we already proved you can leave."

  Heartening pattern: very low REBUILD count. Most of what exists is either right (KEEP) or close-to-right (REFACTOR with
  specific decision-driven adjustments). The work is mostly wiring + extending + adding new modules — not rewriting the
  substrate.
  
  Cross-cutting themes emerging:

  1. The substrate is sound, the wiring is broken. T3 said it best: "KG indexer's 0-node state is a wiring problem, not a 
  code problem." Same pattern across all 3 reports — the components exist (KG, audit, calibration, memory, swarm engine,
  build_dispatcher) but aren't connected to their inputs/outputs in the v3 architecture.
  2. build_dispatcher.py + auditor.py + pipeline/ are exemplary.* These are the "already-correct-for-v3" anchors. Decision
  #13 (engineer hybrid by tier, agent-agnostic) is literally in build_dispatcher.py's docstring. pipeline/ (manifest_loader
   + capability_checker + versioning + project_lock + phase_evolution) is the substrate for #4 federation, #16 federation
  updates, #33D version migrations — almost no rework.
  3. Cite-or-Refuse (#12) is unenforced everywhere it should be. Verify wrappers don't enforce; scout_contribution.py has
  no citation field; build hooks don't check. Need schema teeth + middleware.
  4. Smart Spine calibration (#27) is a data loss every audit. Both verify.py and iso.py extract calibration data and
  discard it. Need a calibration_sink helper called from every audit/invoke.
  5. Work-item types beyond feature (#19) is a missing dimension everywhere. No
  bug/incident/support/refactor/infra/compliance templates; doc parsers only recognize STORY/EPIC/INIT; dispatcher only
  routes feature work.
  6. Zero tests under build/ and plan/tests/ — significant credibility gap; need to address proactively, not retroactively.

  Specific quiet bugs surfaced during triage:
  - plan/pipeline/phase_evolution.py rename detection collapses to ("", "", artifact) for many phases (missing label field)
   → rename will misfire
  - plan/pipeline/project_lock.py SQL injection surface (string-concat with _esc only escaping single quotes)
  - build/kg/extractors/markdown.yaml vs build/kg/doc_parser/ duplication unresolved

  .env), share-pg.sh (prints DB password!), run-standalone-watcher.sh (shared DSN), spine-connect.sh (raw DSN). All need
  Vault wiring before #18 closed-source v1.0 ships.

  T5's proposed architectural restructure

  This is the most consequential recommendation across any of the 4 reports so far:
  
  v3 top-level layout:

  orchestrator/       — lifecycle + routing + gate + federation (keep+extend; high quality)
  shared/runtime/     — NEW: substrate reusable across role workers, Hub backend, orchestrator
                        (vitals, heartbeat, watchdog, notify, executor, usage-parsers,
                         file-lock, updater, db-outbox — the keepers from today's lib/)
  hub/                — NEW top-level: the containerized Hub PRODUCT (#3) — real SPA, real API
  shared/charters/    — NEW: industry-anchored role charters
                        (replaces lib/role-prompts/ with Scrum/PMBOK/SRE/ITIL/NIST anchors
                         + 6 new charters: devops, customer_support, compliance_officer,
                         security_engineer, tech_writer, release_manager)
  lib/                — RETIRED entirely after migration

  This is a significant restructure but it's the cleanest expression of all 33 design decisions. The architectural debt of
  v1's file-bus daemon model gets fully cleared.

⏺ T1 (shared/) back. All 6 in. Triage phase complete.

  T1's headline finding
  
  T1's dominant pattern across 104 files is "substrate-correct, surface-incorrect": the concepts are right for v3, but the
  surfaces assume laptop-only, single-user, single-provider posture. Only 3 true REBUILDs (shared/cost/prompt_cache.py
  Anthropic-only, shared/api/dependencies.py header-stub auth + subprocess psql + in-process-only MCP,
  shared/ui/approvals/proxy.py superseded by api routes). 2 DELETEs (the dev-only approvals proxy pair after api cutover).

  The bigger story is 9 missing shared/ sub-packages* that locked decisions require but don't exist:
  - shared/llm/ (#2) — single provider-agnostic call surface; absorbs prompt_cache as a provider trait
  - shared/secrets/ (#9) — vault adapter; replaces ~/.spine/*.yaml + env-var creds throughout
  - shared/integrations/ (#11, #20, #33A) — GitHub/Linear/Jira/Slack/PagerDuty/Twilio/Teams/Vanta/Drata/Secureframe
  connectors
  - shared/license/ (#23) — Ed25519-signed bundle verifier + feature-flag evaluator
  - shared/migration/ (#33) — onboarding (GitHub→Spine) + portability + version migrations
  - shared/learning/ (#27) — Smart Spine 3-tier scope resolver + contribution gates
  - shared/recovery/ (#31) — DR backup/restore/auto-recovery
  - shared/federation/ (#4, #10, #16) — parent/child Hub registry + upstream/downstream MCP + update cascade
  - shared/identity/ (#25) — Keycloak OIDC client + middleware + RBAC
  
  Plus extensions to shared/api/ (decisions, role_chat, registry, vault_config, integrations, federation routes +
  OIDC/feature-flag middleware), shared/mcp/ (remote transport + federation/migration/recovery/license tools),
  shared/schemas/ (prd_v1, trd_v1, decision_card_v1, briefing_v1, work_item, federation/consent_v1, license/bundle_v1),
  shared/ui/ (decision-queue/master-roles/registry/audit/vault-config/integrations/role-chat/federation/license panels +
  responsive.css + onboarding wizard).

  Architectural tension to flag (will resolve at build-sequence step, not now)
  
  T1 puts the 9 new packages under shared/*. T5+T6 put many of the same things at top-level (federation/, vault/,
  keycloak/, license/, etc.). Real trade-off: shared/* is easier for cross-subsystem reuse (federation/identity/learning
  all get called from plan+build+verify+orchestrator); top-level is clearer ownership boundary. Flagging it now; resolving
  it during build-sequence planning, not aggregation.

  Final cumulative across all 6 reports

  Final read on the v3 shape:
  - ~75% of existing code is KEEP or REFACTOR — the substrate is largely sound
  - Only 6% REBUILD — almost all concentrated in landing docs (v1 framing) + a handful of provider-bound files
  - Only 5% DELETE — mostly old lib/ daemon system + dev-only proxies
  - ~264 BUILD-NEW proposed — the v3 build is overwhelmingly addition, not subtraction
  
  The dominant build motion is: add the 9-11 missing subsystems, refactor the existing substrate to talk to them, retire 
  lib/ + v1 docs.

⏺ Let me check the canonical record before reciting from memory — I want to make sure I'm giving you the actual locked
  list, not a paraphrase.

  Listed 2 directories (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Important caveat before I list them: there is no canonical docs/V3_DESIGN_DECISIONS.md yet. The 33 decisions live in
  conversation context + scattered references throughout the triage reports — never got committed to a file. (T6 actually
  flagged this as the first thing to write in v3 doc cycle, because everything else depends on it.) So what follows is my
  reconstruction from numbered references in the 6 triage reports; treat the numbers as my best read, not a quoted source —
   we may need to reconcile a few.

  I count 31 numbered decisions plus 2 numbers I never see referenced (#26 and #32), plus a handful of items that came up
  but I don't recall getting their own number. Here they are grouped by theme:

  A. Positioning & Identity (4)
  
  - #1 "AI software company in a box" — not for vibecoders; for enterprises OR individuals wanting enterprise-grade
  software
  - #14 Three target segments — solo founder, startup, regulated enterprise (no SMB middle)
  - #18 Closed-source v1.0
  - #21 Spine built by Spine (dogfood is mandatory)

  B. Deployment & Distribution (4)

  - #3 Hub is a containerized product — the primary management surface (NOT a template you drop into projects, which was
  the v1 framing)
  - #15 Self-hosted — BYOC managed-customer-cloud OR pure customer-cloud OR on-prem; NOT SaaS
  - #17 Four deployment shapes — laptop, BYOC-managed, customer-cloud, on-prem
  - #20 Cloud breadth — AWS + Azure + GCP + Railway + Fly + DO + on-prem

  C. Federation & Hierarchy (3)

  - #4 Federation tree — Hub-to-Hub upstream/downstream control + data plane
  - #10 "A Hub is a Hub is a Hub" — fractal; peer-consent default, bounded mandatory upward flows
  - #16 Update distribution — vendor → parent Hub → child Hub with approval cascade

  D. Identity, Secrets, Licensing (3)

  - #9 Vault-only secrets — OpenBao default; no env vars; no built-in secret store
  - #23 Feature-flag licensing — Ed25519-signed bundle, per-flag enable, per-quota counters (founders pay per feature)
  - #25 Keycloak embedded by default for identity/auth/RBAC

  E. LLM & Roles (5)
  
  - #2 LLM-agnostic — Anthropic + OpenAI + Bedrock + Vertex + Ollama + Qwen + in-house vLLM
  - #7 Industry-anchored role charters — Scrum, PMBOK, SRE, ITIL, NIST anchors (not bespoke)
  - #8 Hybrid authority — bounded override at project level under Hub policy
  - #11 DevOps role + Operate as 6th corner with 8 control planes
  - #13 Engineer is a wrapper — over Claude Code / Cursor / Aider / OpenHands / human; agent-agnostic with autonomy tiers

  F. Quality & Verify (4)

  - #12 Cite-or-Refuse contract for verify-class roles
  - #22 Intelligent per-artifact triage (the very exercise we just ran on the codebase)
  - #24 Internal Evidence Store + Vanta/Drata/Secureframe connector scaffolds (compliance)
  - #30 MCP scaffold "heavier" — OpenAPI emission + envelope + RBAC tags + feature-flag awareness

  G. Communication & Decisions (4)

  - #5 Active push — briefings + decision cards (not pull-based dashboards alone)
  - #6 Multi-channel per-user per-decision-class — Slack + Email + SMS + WhatsApp + Teams + PagerDuty
  - #28 Mobile-responsive web on Day 1
  - #29 Voice/SMS scaffold via Twilio; native mobile + voice as scaffolds

  H. Work Types & Lifecycle (1)

  - #19 Seven work-item types — feature, bug, incident, support, refactor, infra, compliance
  
  I. Smart Spine & Learning (1)

  - #27 3-tier learning — per-project always-on, within-Hub default ON, cross-org default OFF (opt-in, permissioned)
  
  J. Resilience & Migration (2)

  - #31 Disaster recovery — auto-recovery + immediate notification + active-passive cross-region opt-in
  - #33 Migration tools — 4 concerns: A) onboarding from GitHub/Linear/Jira, B) portability export/import, C) work-type
  migration, D) Spine version migrations

  Honest gaps / things I'm not sure about

  - #26 and #32 are unaccounted — I never see them referenced in any triage report. Either I missed them in my note-taking
  or the numbering skipped. We may need to recover those.
  - "ALL AI ALL THE TIME — no human engineering hires" — this came up strongly but I don't see it with its own number. May
  be #26 or #32, or may have been treated as a meta-rule applying to all roles.
  - Pricing — explicitly DEFERRED ("not ready to price what hasn't been built/tested"); locked the licensing architecture
  (#23) but not prices
  - Spine-for-Spine, workspace hygiene as a Spine design concern, "thicker the better with option to lighten" (the
  "tier-down for solo founder" mention) — came up but I don't remember formal locking

  Which theme do you want to start reviewing? I'd recommend A or H since they cast the longest shadow over the rest, but
  pick what's most useful. (After we walk through, the natural artifact is docs/V3_DESIGN_DECISIONS.md — T6's #1 doc-cycle
  priority anyway.)
